statements.statement: In meetings when someone challenges my idea, I feel this rapid cycling — first a sharp competitive flare, then a fog of self-doubt, then sometimes a quiet clarity underneath both. I've learned to wait for that third thing instead of speaking from the first two.

Some mornings I wake up dull and heavy, other mornings restless and buzzy, and occasionally just clean and still. I used to think whichever one showed up was 'me' for the day — now I notice I'm the one registering which one arrived, and that noticing part doesn't change with the rest.

When my partner gets short with me, I can feel two reactions fighting — one wants to fire back, one wants to go silent and withdraw. I've started just watching that tug-of-war without picking a side, and usually something calmer surfaces on its own.

Around 3pm my energy crashes and I get this irritable urge to snap at whoever messages me next. I can feel the heaviness pushing out the clarity I had this morning. I just notice it now, like watching weather move through, and I don't act on the irritation.

At my son's baseball games, I notice this pull to compare him to the other kids and a simultaneous urge to just not care. Both impulses are loud but I've gotten good at sitting there and letting them argue without me joining in — I just watch him play.

On the commute home, I can feel myself switching between agitation about the day and a sluggish nothing-matters feeling. I've started treating both like passengers in the car rather than the driver. By the time I pull into the driveway, neither one is running the show.

At neighborhood barbecues I notice myself toggling between wanting to be the entertaining one and wanting to disappear into the corner. Now I just register both pulls without obeying either, and I end up finding a comfortable middle — talking to one person at a time, staying easy.

When I'm doing dishes and my mind starts its usual argument — part of me insisting I should be more productive, another part whispering nothing matters — I've learned there's a spot in me that just hears them both and keeps rinsing plates. That spot is what holds my evenings together.

During my annual physical, the doctor asks how I'm doing and I say fine, but inside I notice this flicker of anxiety competing with a heavy indifference. I can watch them both without performing either one. That watching is what lets me answer honestly.

When my sister calls crying about her divorce, I feel a rush to fix it and simultaneously a pull to shut down. I've learned to let both of those pass through me without grabbing either one, and what comes out is just steady presence — I ask her what she needs instead of reacting.

I spend so much energy watching my own internal shifts that I've stopped participating in things. My wife says I've become a spectator in our marriage — present but never actually in it, always observing from behind glass.

At work I can track every micro-change in my mood during a presentation — the nervousness, the confidence, the dullness — but I'm so absorbed in monitoring all of it that I lose my place in the slides. My manager asked if I was distracted.

I've gotten so used to watching my reactions without acting on them that I can't make simple decisions anymore. Even choosing what to eat for lunch becomes this endless internal observation exercise. My friend waited fifteen minutes at the counter while I just stood there noticing my own indecision.

When my kids fight at the dinner table, I notice myself cataloging my own responses — irritation, then fatigue, then detachment — instead of actually intervening. They need a parent and I'm just sitting there being an audience to my own inner theater.

My therapist said I intellectualize everything by turning it into an observation about my own states. Even grief — when my dad died, I kept noting how sadness and numbness alternated inside me, and I never actually cried. I watched myself not crying and noted that too.

I've become so detached from whatever's churning inside me that people at parties say I seem hollow. I can describe exactly what's moving through me — the anxiety, the boredom, the flash of interest — but none of it reaches my face or voice.

My boyfriend says I narrate my own feelings instead of feeling them. When we argue, I'll say something like 'I notice anger arising and also a desire to withdraw' and he's like, just be angry or be sad, stop commentating. But I can't turn off the watching.

At the gym I track my motivational state so closely — am I driven today or sluggish, am I forcing it or flowing — that I rarely just lift. I'm always one step removed, monitoring which mode I'm in, and my trainer says I'm overthinking every rep.

During team brainstorms, I'm so busy watching the interplay between my competitive impulse and my lazy impulse that I barely contribute ideas. I leave meetings having observed myself beautifully but having added nothing.

I walk through the farmers' market noticing my inner states shift — wanting, then boredom, then a little burst of pleasure — and I realize I've circled the whole market without buying anything or actually enjoying it. The watching has eaten the experience.

When my coworker snaps at me, I'm completely inside the anger before I know what happened. There's no gap between the trigger and my reaction — I've already fired off a sharp reply and slammed my laptop shut. I only realize what I did five minutes later.

I wake up foggy and just assume that's who I am today. It doesn't occur to me that the fog might shift or that there's a part of me separate from it. I cancel my plans, tell my roommate I'm out, and go back to bed believing this is just how it is.

At family dinners when my mom criticizes my job, I either blow up or go completely silent for the rest of the night. I wish there were some middle place but when the feeling hits, it's just me — there's no distance at all between the emotion and what I do next.

If I'm restless, I'm completely restless — I can't sit still, I text everyone, I start three projects. If I'm heavy, I'm completely heavy — I lie on the couch and can't answer my phone. Whatever state comes, it just takes me over and I'm it until it passes.

My partner says I have no self-awareness in the moment. She can see my mood shifting before I can. By the time I notice, I've already ruined the evening — snapped at her or checked out entirely. There's no catching it while it's happening.

During video calls, if someone says something that triggers my competitive streak, I'm already talking over them before I realize I got activated. I couldn't tell you what happened in my body or mind — it's all one fast blur from trigger to action.

On days when I feel driven and sharp, I over-commit to everything — extra projects, social plans, promises to my kids. I don't notice that this driven state is temporary. Then it crashes and I can't follow through, and everyone's disappointed again.

Standing in line at the grocery store, if someone cuts ahead of me, I'm flooded with this hot irritation and I say something sarcastic before thinking. There's no space between what rises in me and what comes out of my mouth. I only regret it in the car.

When I'm in a lazy mood, I genuinely believe nothing matters and that ambition is pointless. When I'm in a driven mood, I think lazy people are wasting their lives. I flip between these completely and each time I'm sure the current one is the real me.

My friend pointed out that I go through the same cycle every week — Monday I'm on fire, Wednesday I'm anxious and scattered, Friday I'm checked out. I'd never seen the pattern. I thought each day was just whatever it was, not something happening to me that I could step back from.

When my mother-in-law rearranges my kitchen during her visit, I feel a sharp irritation and underneath it a dull resignation. I can hold both without reacting to either — I just make tea and let them settle. By the time she asks if I mind, I can answer from somewhere steady.

On the crowded morning train, I notice my body cycling between agitation at being pressed against strangers and a heavy sleepiness pulling me under. There's a part of me that just registers both states without becoming either one, and that's what gets me to work in a usable mood.

During festival preparations, my aunties argue about who's cooking what, and I feel pulled between wanting to assert myself and wanting to just disappear into my phone. I've learned to sit with both impulses and let them pass. Eventually I just start chopping vegetables from a calm place.

When my father lectures me about career choices at dinner, something in me burns with defiance while another part just goes numb and wants to agree. These days I can watch both responses happening in my chest and throat without acting from either — I ask him a question instead, from somewhere quieter.

After a long day at the office where my senior took credit for my work, I notice rage alternating with a flat defeated feeling on the bus home. I've gotten familiar enough with this seesaw that I don't text anything angry or sink into despair. I just watch it rock back and forth until my stop.

Some mornings during prayer, I'm restless and planning my day inside my head; other mornings I can barely keep my eyes open. Occasionally there's a clean stillness. I've stopped judging which state shows up — I just notice which one came and sit with it. The noticing itself feels like the real practice.

When my grandmother compares me to my cousin who just got married, I feel the sting of shame and simultaneously a hot wanting to prove myself. I've started recognizing these as two forces pushing me around rather than my actual self. Watching them contend gives me just enough room to smile and change the subject.

At the wet market early morning, my body sometimes feels alive and alert, other times sluggish and reluctant. I used to force myself through the sluggish days or waste the alert ones. Now I just note which quality is present, move with it rather than against it, and my mornings are smoother.

During my colleague's farewell dinner, I felt myself toggling between genuine warmth and a restless impatience to leave. I didn't force either one — I just noticed the pull in both directions and stayed put. The evening ended naturally, and I left without regret or exhaustion.

When the household help doesn't show up and my mother starts fretting, I feel her anxiety trying to become mine and also a heaviness that says 'just leave it.' I can hold both without being swept into either — I calmly start the work myself while she calms down watching me not panic.

I've become so detached watching my own inner states that my wife says I'm like talking to a wall. She cries about her mother's health and I'm internally noting 'sadness is arising in me, then resistance, then flatness' — but nothing reaches my hands or voice. She needs me to hold her and I'm just observing.

My team leader asks for my opinion and I freeze because I'm tracking three different impulses in myself — the eager one, the cautious one, the indifferent one — and by the time I've observed all three, the meeting has moved on. My seniors think I have nothing to contribute.

At the family dinner table with my parents and in-laws, everyone is talking over each other. I can feel irritation, submission, and numbness all cycling through me — and I watch them so carefully that I become invisible at the table. My father asks if I'm unwell because I haven't spoken in thirty minutes.

During ancestor remembrance rituals, I should feel reverence but I'm so busy monitoring what I actually feel — guilt? obligation? genuine love? — that I miss the entire ceremony. My aunt whispers that I look distracted and it embarrasses my mother.

My best friend confides in me about her marriage troubles and I can map exactly what's happening inside me — concern layered with judgment layered with fatigue — but I can't seem to actually respond with warmth. She told me later I seemed cold. I wasn't cold, I was watching myself too closely to be present.

On festival mornings when the whole family is busy with preparations, I stand in the kitchen observing my own resistance and eagerness alternating, but I don't actually do anything useful. My mother has to assign me tasks directly because I won't move on my own — I'm stuck in the watching.

My manager gave me feedback and I spent the entire afternoon tracking my internal response — the shame, then the defensiveness, then the collapse — so carefully that I forgot to actually do anything about the feedback. The work sat there untouched while I observed myself being affected by it.

I've gotten so good at watching my own reactions that I can't commit to feeling anything fully. Even at my brother's wedding, I was internally narrating — excitement, boredom, warmth, restlessness — instead of dancing. My cousin said I looked like I was somewhere else entirely.

In the crowded temple during evening prayer, I spend the whole time noting how my attention drifts — devotion, then impatience, then drowsiness — and I never once actually pray. I leave feeling emptier than when I arrived, because I watched everything but participated in nothing.

When neighbours come over unannounced and my mother expects me to serve tea, I feel the pull of duty and resentment and fatigue all at once, and I observe them so thoroughly that I just stand there. My mother has to call my name twice. I'm present in theory but absent in practice.

When my mother scolds me about coming home late, the shame hits and I'm already crying before I can think. There's no space between her words and my reaction — I'm just inside the feeling completely, and whatever I say next comes from that raw place.

If my senior at work raises his voice, I go silent instantly — everything in me shuts down and I can't access anything except this heavy blankness. I don't notice it as a state that came over me; it just feels like I disappeared and won't come back until I'm alone again.

During family gatherings when relatives ask about marriage, I feel a burning in my face and I either laugh too loudly or leave the room. I can't tell what's happening inside me in those moments — it's just one big uncomfortable wave that moves me before I know what I'm doing.

On restless nights before exams, I pace the room and can't stop. I'm not aware that restlessness is a state passing through — it's just completely me. My roommate says 'just sit down' and I literally cannot. The agitation is everything, wall to wall.

When my father-in-law dismisses my opinion at the dinner table, I smile and agree outwardly but inside I'm boiling. I stay boiling for hours after, replaying it in bed. It never occurs to me that the boiling is something separate from me that could be watched — it just is me, stuck.

On mornings when I feel dull and heavy after a late-night family gathering, I believe the heaviness completely. I skip my morning walk, eat poorly, and speak curtly to everyone in the house. I don't realize until the next day that it was just a passing state — while it's there, it's total.

Whenever my colleague gets praised by our boss, a sharp jealousy takes over and I spend the rest of the day undermining him in small ways. I don't catch myself doing it. My friend at work had to point out the pattern three times before I even believed her.

At the temple festival, when the crowd pushes and someone steps on my foot, anger explodes out of me — I shove back and shout before I know it. My wife pulls me aside, horrified. I can't explain what happened; it was like the anger was me for those seconds, no separation at all.

My grandmother says I have my grandfather's temper — hot one minute, dead silent the next. I swing between these without seeing the swing. Each state feels permanent while I'm in it, and I make promises or threats from inside each one that I can't keep once it passes.

When the bus is late and I'm standing in the heat, an anxious urgency takes over — I keep checking my phone, shifting my weight, sighing loudly. I'm not watching myself get agitated; I'm just agitated, completely fused with it, and I snap at the stranger who bumps my bag.

In meetings I notice my appetite to jump in — not to dominate, but because solutions are forming before people finish describing the problem. I have to pace myself, but the impulse itself feels clean, like genuine eagerness rather than nervousness.

When I'm cooking dinner for people coming over, I'm moving fast but not frantic — chopping, tasting, adjusting the heat — and there's this pleasure in the coordination of it all. I don't feel depleted afterward, just warm and satisfied.

I can tell my body actually wants exercise — not in a punishing way, but the way you want to stretch after sitting too long. On those days I'll go hard at the gym and then genuinely want to stop, and sleep comes easily that night. The drive has a natural off-switch.

After my morning coffee I get this window where my body wants to move into the hard tasks — I'll knock out three or four things on my list and my muscles feel warm, not wired. It's like my energy has somewhere useful to land.

When my partner and I are planning a trip together, I feel this pull to research routes, book things, build the itinerary — not because I need control but because the organizing itself feels pleasurable, like a puzzle my hands want to solve.

At neighborhood barbecues I notice I'm the one who ends up flipping burgers, refilling ice, checking on people's drinks. It's not obligation — there's a hum in my chest that actually enjoys keeping things moving, and I still have energy for conversation after.

On my commute home I'm already mentally sorting what to tackle that evening — laundry, my kid's school form, that email I owe my brother. But it doesn't feel heavy. It's more like I'm lining up dominoes I actually want to tap over.
I wake up most mornings with a kind of readiness — not anxious, just ready. My feet hit the floor and there's already a direction. On the days I don't have that, something feels genuinely off.

When a friend calls upset, I notice part of me is already working the problem — who could help, what steps to suggest. I've learned to listen first, but that engine in me is idling the whole time, wanting to be useful.

I got back into running after my second kid was born, and what surprised me was how my whole week organizes around those three morning runs. Not just fitness — my patience at work, my sleep, even my appetite all calibrate around whether I moved that day.

I haven't had a full day off in probably six weeks. I tell myself this weekend I'll rest, but Saturday morning the list is already in my head and my body won't settle on the couch. By ten a.m. I'm reorganizing the garage and I can feel my jaw is tight.

My wife says I make her feel like she's another item on my to-do list. The thing is, when I'm with her I can feel part of my brain still running the work problem, and I can't turn it off. My hands are literally fidgeting under the table.

I took on a second freelance project even though the first one already has me up past midnight. My stomach burns after dinner and I'm grinding my teeth in my sleep — my dentist noticed before I did. But the thought of saying no felt worse than the exhaustion.

At my kid's soccer game I'm answering emails on my phone, then I catch myself and put it away, but within five minutes I'm mentally drafting the quarterly report. My son scored and I missed it. I felt sick about it but still couldn't stop.

I went to a friend's birthday party and couldn't just be there. I kept thinking about all the things I could be finishing instead, and then I started feeling irritated at everyone for being so relaxed. My shoulders were up around my ears the whole time.

Even on vacation I'm mapping out the next day by nine p.m. — museums, restaurants, optimal routes. My partner finally told me it feels like a work trip with me. I know she's right but sitting still at a beach makes my skin crawl.

I set three alarms and still check my phone twice before sleeping because I'm afraid of oversleeping and losing productive hours. My heart races if I think I've wasted a morning. Rest doesn't feel earned unless I've crossed enough off.

My colleague asked if I wanted to grab coffee and I said yes but brought my laptop. I didn't mean to be rude — I genuinely cannot sit in a café without doing something. My leg bounces the whole time if my hands are empty.

I've started snapping at my roommate over small things — dishes left out, music too loud — because everything that slows me down feels like it's stealing time. I can feel the irritation building in my chest before I even open my mouth.

I went to the doctor because my resting heart rate was high even though I'm fit. She asked about stress and I said I wasn't stressed, just busy. But when I'm honest with myself, my body hasn't fully relaxed in months — even my breathing is shallow and fast.

I have things I want to do — projects, calls I should make, a resume I need to update — but when I sit down to start, there's nothing there. No spark, no pull. It's not sadness exactly, it's more like the motor just won't turn over.

My friends keep inviting me to things and I keep saying maybe. It's not that I don't want to see them — I just can't find the push to get dressed and drive there. I end up on the couch, not even enjoying the quiet.

At work I used to be the one volunteering for new projects. Now I sit through meetings hoping no one calls on me. The ideas still form vaguely but they dissolve before I can grab them. My manager asked if everything's okay and I didn't know what to say.

Mornings are the worst. The alarm goes off and I just lie there. Not sleepy — awake but flat. There's no reason pulling me out of bed, no appetite for what the day holds. It takes me an hour to even make coffee.

I watch my partner handle the kids, the groceries, the scheduling, and I feel this gap between what I should be doing and what I can actually bring myself to do. My limbs feel heavy, like moving through something thick.

I signed up for a half-marathon six months ago thinking the deadline would motivate me. I haven't run once. The shoes are by the door and I look at them every morning and just walk past. There's no resistance exactly — just absence of pull.

My neighbor asked me to help move a couch on Saturday and I said sure, but when the time came I couldn't make myself go over. It wasn't that I was tired — I'd slept ten hours. It's like initiative has gone missing and I don't know where it went.

I used to cook elaborate meals on weekends — it was my thing. Now I stare at the fridge and close it. I eat cereal or order delivery. The desire to create something, even something small, has just gone quiet.

At a networking event last week I stood near the snack table the whole time, not talking to anyone. I could see opportunities — people I should introduce myself to — but my body wouldn't move toward them. I left early feeling nothing, not even disappointment.

Transitioning from the weekend into Monday used to be easy — I'd feel a little surge Sunday night, planning the week. Now Monday comes and I drag myself through it like I'm pulling a weight. There's no forward lean, just obligation holding me upright.

When I'm preparing lunch boxes for my children in the morning — cutting fruit, stacking containers, wiping spills — my hands move quickly and there's a rhythm to it that feels good. I'm done before my mother-in-law even finishes her tea, and I still have energy to chat with her before I leave.

During festival preparation I'm the one organizing — which relatives bring what, who needs pickup from the station, how the rooms should be arranged. It doesn't drain me. I feel the most like myself when I'm holding all those threads and watching them come together.

At work my team lead assigns me the projects with tight deadlines because she knows I'll run at them. I feel my metabolism speed up — appetite strong, mind clear, body alert. When I hand it in early, the satisfaction sits in my chest like warmth.

On the train home I'm already thinking about what to tackle — the plumber who needs calling, my son's tuition form, the vegetables that need buying before the market closes. But it's not weight. It's more like a current I ride comfortably.

When my father asks me to handle his insurance paperwork, I feel something like eagerness — not because I love paperwork, but because solving things for people I care about gives me a physical sense of purpose. My focus sharpens and I don't notice time passing.

My neighbor asked for help organizing the colony's New Year gathering. Within an hour I'd made a budget, called the caterer, and drafted a notice. My wife laughed and said I can't help myself, but honestly my body felt settled afterward — like the energy had been used well.

I exercise every morning before anyone else wakes — just thirty minutes of skipping or bodyweight sets in the balcony. My appetite is strong at breakfast, I'm sharp in the office, and by night I'm genuinely tired. It all links together and the day holds its shape.

When I'm bargaining with a vendor at the market — comparing quality, checking prices at the next stall, calculating per-kilo value in my head — I notice a kind of quiet aliveness. It's small, but my mind is engaged and my body feels springy. I come home feeling competent.

My sister called with a problem about her daughter's school admission and before she'd finished explaining, solutions were already forming in my mind — which contacts to try, what documents to gather. I held back and listened, but the readiness to act felt like warmth in my palms.

After working all day I still find myself wanting to rearrange the prayer shelf or fix the loose kitchen handle before sitting down. My grandmother says I'm like my grandfather — always busy, always building. It doesn't feel forced. It feels like the most natural state for my hands.

I cannot stop checking my work phone even during family dinner. My mother keeps telling me to eat properly, and I can see the hurt on her face, but my mind is already on tomorrow's presentation. My neck is stiff by the time I get up from the table.

I volunteered to coordinate my cousin's wedding even though I'm already behind on my own deadlines. Now I'm up until two a.m. making vendor calls, and my body is running on tea and irritation. If anyone suggests I delegate, I feel a flash of anger in my chest.

My father told me to slow down — that I'm becoming short-tempered with the household help over nothing. I know he's right. Everything that doesn't move at my speed feels like an obstacle, and I can feel the heat climbing behind my eyes before I snap.

I took three tuition classes alongside my degree because being idle terrifies me. My appetite has become erratic — I forget lunch and then eat too much at night. My roommate says I pace the room even while on the phone. I can't sit without shaking my leg.

During Lunar New Year I took over the entire preparation — cleaning, shopping, decorating, cooking — and refused help from my sisters-in-law. By the day itself I was so exhausted I snapped at my children for being too loud. My hands were trembling while serving food.

At the office I've become the person who stays latest, and I feel secretly resentful of colleagues who leave on time. My stomach is acidic most evenings and I've started getting headaches behind my right eye. But resting while there's work left feels physically wrong.

My wife says I've turned our children's education into a project I'm managing — tracking their scores, scheduling extra coaching, comparing with cousins' results. She says they're scared of me now. I feel the truth of it in my gut but the drive to push won't quiet down.

Even at the temple, my mind is listing what needs to be done after. I'm folding my hands but planning the week behind my closed eyes. My body won't settle — my knees ache from bouncing, and I leave before the final prayers because the stillness is unbearable.

I've been waking at four a.m. without an alarm — not refreshed, but wired. My body feels hot even in the air-conditioning. I go straight to my desk and start working, and by mid-morning I'm irritable and my hands shake slightly when I pour tea.

Neighbors have started avoiding me because whenever we talk I'm rushing — cutting conversations short, tapping my foot, glancing at my watch. I know it's rude but the sense of urgency in my chest won't let me be still with people. I've started taking a longer route home just to avoid small talk.

My mother cooks all the meals now even though she's seventy, because I can't make myself get up and do it. She doesn't say anything but I see her face. I used to be the one running the household. Now I sit and watch her and something inside just will not move.

My boss assigned me a straightforward report two weeks ago and I haven't started. I sit at my desk and open the file and stare at it. There's no resistance, no fear — just blankness. My colleagues are moving around me and I feel like I'm watching from behind glass.

Everyone in my family is preparing for my brother's wedding — stitching clothes, making calls, visiting. They keep asking me to help and I say I will, but then I lie down and hours pass. My body feels like it belongs to someone else. I can't find the switch.

I used to walk to the market every evening, pick fresh vegetables, chat with the aunties on the way. Now I order everything delivered and still can't bring myself to unpack the bags when they arrive. They sit by the door until my husband puts them away.

My children ask me to play and I tell them later. Later never comes. I'm not doing anything else — just sitting with my phone, scrolling without reading. There's no pull toward anything, not even rest. Just a kind of stalled engine in my chest.

At the office gathering everyone was talking, networking, exchanging cards. I stood near the door with a plate of food I wasn't eating. People smiled at me and I smiled back but couldn't push past that into actual conversation. It wasn't shyness — there was just nothing behind the smile wanting to go anywhere.

My grandmother keeps telling me that at my age she was raising four children and running a shop. I know she means it lovingly, but when I compare myself I feel nothing — not shame, not anger, just emptiness. Like the engine she's describing was never installed in me.

I haven't cleaned my flat properly in weeks. The dust is visible. My aunt visited and I could see her noticing, and still I felt no urgency to change it. My limbs feel dense and slow, and starting anything feels like trying to push a boulder uphill from a sitting position.

On the bus to work I used to review my day's plan, feel that little charge of anticipation. Now I just stare out the window. My stop comes and I have to force my legs to stand. The commute used to energize me — now it's just time spent being still without wanting to be.

My friend asked me to join her morning walk — she even offered to come to my building. I agreed but when morning came I couldn't get out of bed. Not sleepy, not sick. Just flat. I texted sorry and lay there looking at the ceiling fan, feeling nothing about having cancelled.

trigunas\trajas_projects_tamas_veils_sat.display_name: Rajas Projects Tamas Veils Sattva Reveals

statements.statement: In group conversations at work, I've started noticing when my eagerness to speak is just momentum pushing outward, versus when I'm holding back out of fog. There's a third thing — a simple readiness — and I wait for that before I open my mouth.

My diet used to swing between grabbing anything stimulating — extra coffee, hot sauce on everything — and then crashing into wanting only heavy comfort food and sleep. Lately there's a middle signal, almost quiet, where my body asks for something specific and simple, and following it leaves me feeling transparent instead of wired or dull.

With my sister, I used to either fire off a defensive text within seconds or go silent for days. Now I notice both impulses as separate forces — the heat and the shutdown — and I wait for the moment when I can actually see what she meant. The reply writes itself from there.

When I sit down to plan my week on Sunday evenings, I can tell which urges are just restlessness wanting to fill the calendar, which ones are me avoiding something by leaving it blank, and which commitments actually light up as clear and right. I sort from there.

At the gym, I used to push harder whenever I felt agitated and skip entirely when I felt flat. Now I check which one is running the show before I decide what to do. Most mornings, there's a quieter signal underneath both, and if I follow that one my body actually recovers.

My neighbour talks a lot and I used to either get pulled into her drama or tune out completely and feel guilty. Lately I just stand there and something in me stays still enough that I can hear what she actually needs without getting swept up or shutting down.

When I walk into a networking event, I notice the part of me that wants to work the room and collect cards, and the part that wants to leave immediately. I've learned to wait until something genuinely interests me — a person, a question — and move toward that. The evening feels clean afterward.

Driving home from work, there's a stretch of highway where I used to either race through traffic or zone out so badly I'd miss my exit. Now I notice which pull is stronger and something in me settles between them. I arrive home actually present instead of wound up or half-asleep.

If my kids are fighting and my partner looks at me expecting me to intervene, I can feel the rush to fix it and also the urge to just leave the room. I've started recognizing a third place — calmer, clearer — where I can actually see what's happening and respond to what's needed.

Before a big presentation, I notice the buzzy adrenaline that wants me to over-prepare and also a heavy pull toward procrastination. I've learned that neither one produces my best work. There's a window — usually early morning — where I think clearly without either force running things, and I protect that time.

I cannot stop adding things to my to-do list. Every time I finish something, three more ideas push forward before I've even sat down. My body is buzzing by evening and sleep won't come because the next plan is already forming.

When my partner suggests a quiet weekend, something in me panics. I fill every hour — errands, calls, projects — because stopping feels like sinking into a pit I can't climb out of. I know I'm exhausting both of us but the momentum won't let me stop.

In meetings I'm the one who always volunteers for the next project before the last one is done. My manager praised it at first but now she's asking if I'm okay. My jaw is clenched all day and my shoulders won't drop even in the shower.

At brunch with friends, I dominate the conversation with plans and opinions. Afterward in the car I feel this hollow spin — like all that talking was just energy throwing itself outward and none of it actually connected me to anyone.

I started marathon training to channel the restlessness but now I can't take a rest day without feeling like I'm failing. My knees hurt, my sleep is worse, but every morning the drive to run is louder than the pain. It's like something in me only knows forward.

Even on vacation I'm checking email, researching restaurants, optimizing the itinerary. My wife says I'm not actually there. She's right — my mind is always projecting into the next thing and I can't feel the beach under my feet right now.

When my teenage son is struggling with homework, I jump in and take over before he even asks. I can feel the push in my chest to solve it, fix it, move it forward. He shuts down and I realize I've made him feel invisible, but I couldn't stop the surge.

I reorganize my apartment every few weeks — furniture, closets, pantry. Friends joke about it but they don't feel what I feel: this constant forward-leaning pressure that needs an outlet or I'll snap at the next person who talks to me.

At the grocery store I'm already composing emails in my head, planning dinner, thinking about tomorrow's deadline. I get home and realize I bought duplicates of things I already had because I wasn't actually seeing the shelves. The drive just keeps projecting ahead.

I signed up for three online courses this month. I haven't finished one. Each time I start, a new possibility feels more urgent and I pivot. My browser has forty tabs open and my chest feels tight all the time, like I'm sprinting while standing still.

I know something is off because I can't tell the difference between resting and avoiding anymore. My whole weekend disappears into the couch and I don't feel restored — just thick, like my thinking has a layer of gauze over it.

My friend invites me to things and I say yes, but when the time comes I feel a heavy pull to cancel. It's not anxiety exactly — it's more like I can't see the point. The fog between me and doing anything is so dense nothing seems worth the effort.

At work, my manager asks what I want for my career and I go blank. Not because I'm thinking — because there's nothing there. No pull forward, no clarity, just a flatness that makes the question feel irrelevant.

I eat the same bland food every day because choosing something feels like too much. My roommate asks if I want to try the new Thai place and something in me just goes heavy and says no without checking whether I actually want to go.

My mother calls and I let it ring. It's not that I'm angry — I just can't gather enough of myself together to have a conversation. By the time I think about calling back, days have passed and the gap feels normal now.

When I try to read a book in the evenings, my eyes move over the words but nothing registers. I read the same paragraph three times and it stays outside me. I put the book down and stare at the wall and even that feels the same — no difference between engaging and not.

In team meetings, I hear people get excited about ideas and I can't find that gear in myself. It's not disagreement — it's like the part of me that would have a direction or preference is asleep. I just agree with whatever seems to have the most energy in the room.

My doctor asked if I'm depressed and I said I don't think so — I'm not sad, exactly. It's more like everything has the same weight and color. I can't distinguish what matters from what doesn't. It all just sits there equally flat.

On mornings when my alarm goes off, there's no resistance and no motivation — just a blankness that holds me in bed. I'm not sleeping but I'm not deciding to stay either. Eventually I get up because enough time has passed, not because something in me shifted.

I walk through my neighbourhood and pass the same houses, the same people, and none of it registers as familiar or interesting. My neighbour waves and I wave back but there's no warmth in it — not coldness either, just nothing. Like I'm behind thick glass.

When my mother-in-law starts giving opinions on how I should cook, I used to either argue back immediately or just go quiet and stew. Now I notice the heat and the shutdown as two separate pulls, and underneath there's a still place where I can see she's just trying to feel useful. I respond from there.

During morning puja at home, I can tell whether I'm going through the motions out of habit — a kind of dullness where my mind is empty but not clear — or rushing through to get to work. On good mornings, there's a quality of attention that is neither sleepy nor hurried, and the ritual actually lands in me.

At family dinners with my parents and brother's family, the conversation gets loud and competitive. I notice the urge to jump in and prove my point and also the pull to just tune out and eat. I wait for the moment when I can actually see what everyone is feeling, and then I know whether to speak or stay quiet.

When my father suggests I take a certain job because of family connections, something in me used to either flare up in rebellion or collapse into obedience. Now I hold both and check — is there something true in his suggestion that my pride is hiding from me, or is it genuinely not right? The answer comes clearly when I wait.

On the crowded MRT, I used to either get agitated by the bodies pressing in or zone out completely on my phone. Lately I find a steadiness that lets me be in the crowd without being pushed or pulled by it. I arrive at my stop actually knowing how I feel instead of depleted or numb.

My grandmother insists on feeding me even when I'm full. I used to snap at her or just eat until I felt sick. Now I can feel the push she creates and the heaviness that wants me to just give in, and I find the gentle place where I can hold her hand and say no without any sharpness.

When colleagues stay late to show dedication, I used to feel the pull to match them or the guilt of leaving on time. Now I check whether staying actually serves the work or just serves the appearance. That quiet check has become how I make most of my decisions at the office.

Cooking for my family on weekends, I notice when I'm throwing in too many flavours because I'm restless, or cooking something bland because I can't be bothered. The meals that come out right happen when I'm attentive but unhurried — tasting, adjusting, present. My husband notices the difference immediately.

During festival preparations, there's always pressure from relatives to do more, spend more, show more. I also feel a laziness that says skip it all. I've found a way to participate that's neither performative nor withdrawn — where the celebration actually means something to me and my children sense that.

When my neighbour auntie asks pointed questions about my marriage prospects, the old reaction was either to snap something sharp or mumble and escape. Now I notice both surges, and there's a place behind them where I can see her concern and answer with honesty that doesn't cost me anything.

I cannot stop comparing my son's grades with my colleague's children. Every exam result sets off a frenzy — new tuition centre, new schedule, new pressure. My son cries at the study table and I feel the push in me that won't let either of us rest until we're ahead.

At office, I reply to emails at 11 pm, not because there's a deadline but because something in me won't settle until every thread is pushed forward. My wife says I bring the office to bed. She's right — my mind keeps projecting into tomorrow's meetings even as I lie next to her.

When relatives visit, I'm already planning the next gathering before this one is over — the menu, the gifts, whether everything reflects well on us. The actual visit becomes just an obstacle between plans. My mother tells me to sit down but my legs won't stop moving.

I took on organizing the temple fundraiser, the children's school committee, and my father-in-law's 70th birthday — all in the same month. Each commitment felt urgent and obvious at the time. Now I'm sleeping three hours a night and snapping at my children for small things because the forward drive won't release.

At hawker centres with friends, I'm checking my phone every minute, thinking about the renovation, the property agent's message, the next investment. They tell me I'm not listening. I'm not — my attention is always throwing itself at whatever problem comes next, and I can't reel it back even for a bowl of laksa.

My parents want me to apply for a government job for stability. Instead I've started three side businesses in six months — none finished. Each new idea burns so bright that I drop the last one. My room is full of half-built plans and my body vibrates with a restlessness that doesn't produce anything complete.

During Deepavali preparations, I cleaned every corner of the house twice, arranged the kolam three times, and rearranged the sweets display until 2 am. My sister said it looked fine hours ago. But the drive to perfect and push forward made me unable to stop — my hands kept moving even when my eyes burned.

I notice at family gatherings I'm always the one rallying everyone — booking the restaurant, settling the bill, organizing the photos. If I sit still for ten minutes, my chest tightens and I feel useless. My cousins call me the engine but lately the engine has no brakes and my digestion is shot from the constant tension.

On the train to work, I'm already rehearsing what I'll say to my manager, drafting proposals in my head, planning how to outperform my peer. By the time I reach the office I've already lived through six scenarios and my jaw is so tight I can barely eat breakfast.

At weddings, I can't just enjoy the ceremony — I'm calculating costs, comparing with our own event, thinking about what we should do better for my daughter's wedding years from now. My wife squeezes my hand to bring me back but the projecting doesn't stop.

My mother prepares elaborate meals and I eat whatever is put in front of me without tasting it. She asks if the rasam needs more tamarind and I say it's fine. I genuinely cannot tell — my tongue and my mind feel equally dull, like a thick cloth is between me and everything.

My supervisor gives me a clear task with a deadline and I stare at the screen for hours. It's not that I'm confused — it's that something in me can't find a reason to move. No resistance, no interest. The cursor blinks and I blink with it.

During Chinese New Year, everyone is visiting relatives, exchanging mandarin oranges, asking about the year ahead. I sit on the sofa at my aunt's house and cannot make myself participate. It's not shyness — it's more like I can't find myself in the room. Everything passes over me like water over a stone.

My father asks me what I want to study and my mind goes blank — not overwhelmed, just empty. My brother has five options; I have none. It's not that I rejected them. It's that nothing appears. Like the part of me that would have a direction hasn't woken up.

In the morning my grandmother does her prayers and calls me to join. I sit next to her but my body feels like it weighs twice what it should. My eyes are open but nothing reaches me — not the incense, not her voice, not the bell. I'm there but sealed off from all of it.

My friends plan trips together and include me in the group chat. I read the messages but typing a reply feels like lifting something impossibly heavy. Days later they ask if I'm okay. I want to explain but even finding the words for what's wrong is too much — there's no wrong, exactly. Just nothing.

At work, my colleagues debate a new policy with passion on both sides. I sit in the meeting without opinion. It's not diplomacy — I genuinely cannot locate a preference. When they ask me directly, I repeat whoever spoke last because I have nothing of my own to offer.

My wife tells me our daughter's school called about her grades and I nod. She waits for a reaction — worry, a plan, something. But inside there's just a thickness that absorbs every piece of information without producing a response. She cries and I watch, feeling nothing move in me.

Neighbours bring food during a festival and I accept it at the door, put it on the table, and forget it's there. Not intentionally — it simply stops existing for me the moment I'm not looking at it. Days later it spoils and I throw it away feeling nothing about the waste.

On my commute home, the bus passes through streets full of light and noise and I register none of it. I arrive at my stop only because the routine carries me — not because I noticed anything along the way. My body is heavy and my mind is not asleep exactly, just covered, like a window painted over.
I notice my body just wakes up before the alarm lately — not groggy, not wired, just ready. There's this easy steadiness through the morning that doesn't require coffee or willpower to maintain.

When someone at work gets sharp with me, I notice I can just hear them without my chest tightening. I end up responding to what they actually need rather than defending myself — it doesn't even feel like effort.

When I'm choosing what to eat, I genuinely want the simpler thing — the bowl of fruit, the plain rice. It's not discipline; my body just feels clearer afterward and I've started trusting that signal without second-guessing it.

In conversations with my sister, I catch myself actually tracking what she means underneath her words — not strategizing my reply, just understanding. Afterward I realize I retained everything she said without trying to memorize it.

I went to a networking event last week and instead of performing or scanning the room, I just talked to people. I left feeling like I'd actually met someone, not like I'd spent energy I'd need to recover from.
My sleep has been this one continuous thing lately — I lie down, I'm out, I wake up and it feels like no time passed but also like I rested fully. I don't remember the last time I needed melatonin.

Driving home from the office, I notice I'm not replaying conversations or rehearsing tomorrow. The road is just the road, and my mind feels like a clean counter — everything put away without me deciding to tidy up.

My kids were fighting this morning and I stepped in without that familiar surge of frustration. I could see what each one needed, said something simple, and it actually landed. My wife looked at me like she was surprised.

At the grocery store, I noticed an older man struggling with bags and I just went over. It wasn't a decision — my body was already moving. The whole interaction took thirty seconds and I felt warm for the rest of the afternoon.

I've been making work decisions faster lately because the right option just seems obvious. Not impulsive — more like the noise around choices has quieted down and what matters is sitting there clearly.

I've started checking my own calmness like it's a performance metric. If I feel even slightly agitated during a meeting, I can't focus on the content anymore — I'm only monitoring whether I've lost my composure.

My partner wants to watch a thriller together and I can't do it. Not because I'm scared — because I can feel it disturbing something in me that took all day to build, and I'd rather sit alone than let that happen.
I turned down a friend's birthday dinner because the restaurant would be loud and late and I'd spend Sunday recovering my equilibrium. She said I've become impossible to make plans with.

I catch myself silently judging my coworkers for how they eat — the break-room pizza, the energy drinks. Something in me recoils and I know it's not fair, but I can't stop the reaction. It feels like their choices are an assault on something I'm trying to protect.

My morning routine has expanded to two hours and if anything interrupts it — my daughter needs a ride, a pipe leaks — I'm brittle the rest of the day. The routine isn't optional; it's the only way I know how to be okay.

I left a dinner party early because someone brought up politics and the energy in the room shifted. Everyone else handled it fine. I sat in my car for ten minutes trying to re-settle before driving home.

My wife says I've become self-righteous about how I live — the early bedtime, the food, the quiet. She's not wrong. I just can't seem to want anything messy anymore, and that's starting to feel like its own problem.

I notice I avoid my brother because his life is chaotic — debt, loud arguments with his wife — and being around him for even an hour costs me two days of feeling contaminated. I know that's not a normal reaction.

At work I've become the person who can't handle ambiguity. I need things orderly and resolved before I can move on. My manager thinks I'm thorough; truthfully I just can't tolerate the static of open questions in my head.

I declined a promotion because the new role would involve conflict and negotiation. Everyone thought I was being humble but really I couldn't risk losing the evenness I've built — it felt like being asked to hand over the only thing that makes me functional.

I used to wake up with this natural readiness — like my body and mind were just aligned. Now it takes three cups of coffee and I still feel like I'm operating through static. I don't know where that clarity went.

My friend asked me a simple question — what do you actually want? — and I couldn't answer. Not because it was deep but because I genuinely couldn't locate anything clear inside. Just fog where a preference should be.
I snap at my kids over small things now and afterward I can't even explain why. It's like my usual buffer — the thing that kept me from reacting — got removed and I'm just raw wires all day.
At the gym I push myself but I never feel clean or light afterward anymore. Just tired and sore. There used to be this sense of everything resetting — like a clear signal — and it's gone.

I used to read a situation in seconds — what people needed, what the right move was. Now I second-guess everything. Meetings end and I replay my own words trying to figure out if I said the right thing.
Food has become purely fuel or comfort — I grab whatever's fast. I remember when my body had opinions about what it wanted, when there was a clean signal. Now it's just noise and cravings.

My neighbor waved at me this morning and I couldn't even produce a genuine smile. I wanted to — she's kind — but something in me feels like it's been turned off. The warmth I used to feel toward people isn't there.
I keep losing things — my wallet, my keys, my train of thought mid-sentence. It's not absent-mindedness; it's like the part of me that used to hold everything in its proper place has gone offline.

Driving to work I cycle through the same three worries on repeat. I used to drive in this comfortable silence — just present, just moving. Now my mind is a hamster wheel and I can't find the off switch.

My wife planned a quiet weekend for us and I couldn't enjoy it. Stillness feels wrong now — like something should be happening, like I should be fixing something. The peace she was offering felt like pressure I couldn't meet.

I wake up with the first light and my body already feels rested — no heaviness, no resistance. By the time I've had warm water and started rice for the family, I notice my mind is already settled, like the day arranged itself before I did anything.

When my mother-in-law gives me instructions about how to cook for the festival, I can just receive it. I don't feel corrected — I feel her care coming through the fussing, and my chest stays open the whole time.
On the crowded bus to work, someone steps on my foot and I notice it registers and passes. There's no story about it. I look at the man and he looks embarrassed and I just nod. My body stays loose.

My father has been unwell and the decisions fall on me — which doctor, which treatment. But when I sit with the options, one rises clearly. My siblings trust what I choose and I trust it too, without needing to justify it endlessly.

After dinner I sit with my grandmother while she listens to devotional songs on the radio. I don't follow the words closely but something in the room — or in me — softens. I sleep deeply those nights.

My colleague at the office takes credit for work I contributed to. I notice I can see it clearly — what happened, what was unfair — but there's no fire in my stomach about it. I decide what to do from a steady place, not a reactive one.

At a family gathering with twenty relatives, I notice I'm not performing or withdrawing. I move between conversations easily. My aunt says I have a calming effect on the room — I don't try to; it just seems to be what happens.

I've been craving lighter food lately — steamed things, dal, plain curd. My mother noticed and started cooking separately for me without my asking. My stomach feels clean and my head feels clear by midday.

Walking home through the market in the evening, I can smell the food stalls and hear vendors calling and children running. Something in me holds all of it without being pulled into any of it. I arrive home still feeling like myself.

When my young son asks me questions — why is the sky that color, why do people lie — I notice answers come without rehearsal. Not clever answers, just true ones. He seems satisfied, and I notice I am too.

I've become so particular about the atmosphere at home — no TV after eight, no heavy food after six, everyone speaking softly — that my husband says it feels like living in a hospital. The children whisper around me and I know that's not right, but I can't tolerate disorder.

When my cousins visit and bring fried snacks and loud laughter, I find myself retreating to my room. I tell myself it's a headache but really their energy feels like an invasion. I used to enjoy them.

My manager wants me to handle a client who shouts and threatens. I keep finding reasons to pass the task to someone else. I tell myself it's strategy but honestly I cannot afford to absorb that — one encounter like that ruins my entire week.

I judge my sister for how she feeds her children — packaged biscuits, sugary drinks. I don't say it but she can feel it. Last Diwali she told me I've become impossible to be around, that I look at her family like they're polluted.

I wake at four and sit in stillness and if anyone in the house makes a sound before five, I feel physically disturbed — my jaw clenches, my morning is ruined. My wife tiptoes around like she's afraid of breaking something.

I refused to attend my uncle's retirement dinner because it was at a restaurant I know serves alcohol. My mother said it's his celebration, not mine, but I couldn't sit there and feel that atmosphere seep into me.

Neighbors invited us for their son's birthday and I made an excuse because I knew there would be loud music and rich food and too many people. My wife went alone. She said the boy asked where uncle was and I felt guilty — but not enough to go.

At work I rewrite emails five times to make sure the tone is perfectly measured. My team waits for responses that should take minutes. I know I'm slowing things down but sending something imprecise feels physically wrong.

I've stopped visiting the temple on festival days because the crowds and noise disturb me more than the visit helps. I go only on empty Tuesday mornings. My father says faith shouldn't be so fragile — I don't know how to explain that it's not faith, it's my whole nervous system.

I turned down an arranged-marriage introduction because the girl's family lives in a joint household with four brothers' families. Just imagining the constant noise and negotiation made something in me shut down. My parents think I'm being unreasonable.

I used to sit down to study or read and everything would go quiet inside — information arranged itself. Now I read the same paragraph four times and nothing sticks. My mother keeps asking what happened to the sharp boy I was.

I eat whatever is put in front of me — heavy curries, sweets from neighbors, leftover rice at midnight. I used to have a sense of what my body wanted. Now it's just filling a hole and feeling sluggish afterward.

At family dinners I pick fights over nothing — my brother's tone, my father's comment about my job. I used to be the one who kept everyone smooth. Now I'm the one creating the tension and I can see them exchanging looks.

My grandmother says I look clouded — that's her word. She touches my forehead like I'm sick. I can't argue with her because I feel it too. Like something that used to be transparent in me has gone murky.

On the train to work I notice I'm playing the same angry scenario in my head — what my colleague said, what I should have said back. By the time I arrive I'm already exhausted and tight across the shoulders. I used to arrive fresh.

My wife asks me to make a simple decision — which school for our daughter, which weekend to visit her parents — and I freeze. Everything feels equally heavy. There's no clear signal, just a thick fog where certainty used to live.

I stopped going for morning walks because I couldn't feel the benefit anymore. The air, the trees, the quiet street — it used to give me something intangible that lasted all day. Now it's just walking and coming back the same.

When a neighbor brings food — which happens often here — I used to feel genuinely grateful, warm in my chest. Now I take the plate, say thank you, and feel nothing. It worries me that the warmth has dried up.

My sleep is broken and full of heavy dreams. I wake up feeling like I haven't rested. My mother says to drink warm milk, my colleague says to try exercise, but the problem feels deeper — like whatever used to reset me overnight has stopped working.

In meetings I used to see through the politics — who was posturing, what the real issue was — and I'd find the right thing to say at the right time. Now I sit there dull, reacting late, and my manager has started speaking for me.
I fall asleep within minutes most nights — my body just drops like a stone. I wake up before my alarm with this heavy, settled feeling in my limbs, like everything got put back in place overnight.

On Sundays I can lie on the couch for two hours doing absolutely nothing and not feel guilty about it. My wife used to think I was depressed, but it's more like my body just needs to go completely offline or Monday doesn't work.

In meetings where everyone's brainstorming and throwing out ideas, I'm the one who can just sit and not contribute for a while. My mind goes quiet and it doesn't bother me — I'll speak when something actually forms, and my manager's stopped mistaking it for disengagement.

When my sister brings up old family stuff at Thanksgiving, I can hear it without my chest tightening. It's like those memories have gotten heavy enough to just stay where they are — they don't float up and hijack me anymore.
After a long week I don't need plans or stimulation — I need a dark room and eight solid hours. That one night of real stillness is what makes me a decent parent on Saturday morning.
I've noticed I'm the only person in my friend group who actually likes being bored. Sitting on the porch watching nothing happen recharges me in a way that hiking or going out never does.

At the gym I don't push for personal records anymore. I do my sets, feel my feet on the floor, and leave feeling heavy and slow in a good way — like my body remembers it's supposed to be solid, not revved up.

When my neighbor's dog died and she came over crying, I didn't try to fix it or say the right thing. I just sat with her on the step. Something in me knows how to be still with hard things without needing to do anything about them.

I protect my bedtime like other people protect their morning coffee. If I let someone talk me into staying up past ten, the next two days are a write-off — not tired exactly, but unmoored, like my base got knocked out.

My commute home is forty minutes and I drive in silence. By the time I pull into the driveway, something in me has gone dense and still — that's the version of me that can actually walk in the door and be present with my kids.
I haven't left my apartment in four days and I can't remember what I ate yesterday. The dishes are stacked up and the thought of washing them feels like someone asking me to lift a car.
My wife keeps asking me what's wrong and I can't answer because nothing happened — I just can't make myself move. The couch has a dent from where I've been sitting since Thursday.
I slept eleven hours last night and woke up feeling like I'd been buried. My limbs are so heavy in the morning that getting to the bathroom feels like wading through wet concrete.

At work I stare at my screen for hours without actually reading anything. My boss sent me a message two days ago and I saw the notification but something in me just won't open it. The longer I wait the heavier it gets.

My friends stopped inviting me to things. I said no so many times that they gave up, and part of me is relieved because even imagining getting dressed and driving somewhere makes my whole body say no.

I eat the same frozen meal every night because choosing something else requires a kind of effort I don't have. My mom called it laziness growing up but it's not that — it's like my brain is wrapped in something thick and nothing gets through clearly.

I missed my son's school play last week because I couldn't get off the bed. I told him I was sick but really I was just lying there, awake, staring at the ceiling, unable to make my body cooperate with what I actually wanted to do.

My desk at home has three months of unopened mail on it. I know some of it is important — bills, maybe something from the IRS — but every time I look at the pile, this fog settles over me and I walk away.

On team calls I keep my camera off and mute myself. I'm there but I'm not — my thoughts move like they're underwater, and by the time I form something to say the conversation has moved on three topics.

I went to the grocery store yesterday and stood in the cereal aisle for fifteen minutes unable to pick one. It's not indecision exactly — it's like my mind is packed in cotton and nothing rises to the level of a preference.

I haven't slept more than four hours in weeks and I can't slow down. Even at midnight my body feels wired and light, like there's no weight in me to pull me toward rest. I keep adding things to my calendar because stillness feels like dying.

My therapist tells me to rest and I literally don't know how. If I sit still for five minutes my leg starts bouncing, my jaw clenches, and I feel this rising panic like I'm wasting something I can't get back.

I run six days a week and when my knee started hurting, my first thought was how to train around it, not how to let it heal. My body doesn't get to just stop — that's not something I know how to allow.
My partner begs me to take a day off with her and I say yes but then I'm checking email the whole time. She says I'm never actually off, and she's right — something in me won't let the engine idle.

At family dinners I'm already thinking about the next thing before dessert is served. My mom says I eat like I'm catching a flight. I can't sit in that after-dinner stillness everyone else seems to enjoy — it makes my skin crawl.
I took a vacation and by day two I was reorganizing the rental cabin's kitchen. My friend laughed and said I don't know how to do nothing. He's right — emptiness feels like a threat, not a gift.

When my colleague told me to take it easy after my surgery, I was back at my laptop within three days. My body was screaming at me but being horizontal with nothing to show for the day felt worse than the pain.

I've never been able to nap. Even as a kid, rest time felt like punishment. Now at forty-three, my doctor says my cortisol is through the roof and I need to learn to be still, but my body has no memory of how that's supposed to feel.

On weekends I fill every hour — farmers market, errands, brunch, cleaning. My neighbor sits on his porch doing nothing and I genuinely don't understand how he tolerates it. The thought of an empty afternoon makes my chest tight.

In meetings I'm already preparing my next point while someone else is still talking. I can't let silence sit — if there's a pause I fill it, because the gap feels unstable, like something will collapse if nobody keeps pushing forward.

After lunch I lie down on the mat in the hall for twenty minutes while my mother-in-law watches the children. My body sinks into the floor and by the time I get up, the afternoon feels possible again — that short stillness is what holds the whole day together.

When the monsoon comes and everything gets damp and slow, I don't fight it. I eat warm rice, sleep early, and let my body be heavy. My husband says I'm the only one in the house who actually matches the season instead of pushing against it.

During big family gatherings I find a corner and just sit. My aunties tease me for being quiet, but I'm not withdrawn — I'm absorbing everything from this still place, and later I'm the one who remembers what everyone said.

I wake at five, but I don't start anything for the first half hour. I sit with my tea in the dark kitchen and let my mind be blank. That emptiness before the household wakes up is what gives me the steadiness to manage everyone's needs all day.

My grandmother taught me that after a death in the family, you sit. You don't fix, you don't cook, you just sit. I do this now — when something ends, I let myself be completely still for a few days before I pick anything up again.

At the office, when deadlines pile up and everyone is frantic, I close my eyes for two minutes at my desk. My colleagues rush past me but those two minutes of complete heaviness reset something — like pressing my body into the chair gives my brain permission to work again.

I know my body needs hot food and early sleep more than most people around me. If I skip dinner or stay up past ten helping my daughter with homework, the next day I'm useless — so I've stopped apologizing for going to bed before everyone else.

On the train home I don't look at my phone. I let my eyes go soft and stare at nothing. By the time I reach my stop, something tight in my shoulders has released and I walk into the flat feeling dense and settled instead of scattered.

When my father raises his voice about my career choices, I don't argue back anymore. Something in me just goes very still and heavy, like a stone dropped into water. It's not fear — it's that I've learned nothing useful comes from me when I'm activated, only when I'm grounded.

During festival preparations when my mother and sisters are rushing around the kitchen, I'm the one sitting and peeling garlic slowly. They call me the tortoise but they also know that when everyone else crashes by evening, I'm the only one still standing — because I never left that slow pace.

I've been sleeping fourteen hours a day and my mother keeps bringing food to my room that I don't touch. The rice goes cold on the tray. She thinks I'm sick but the doctor said nothing's wrong — I just can't find a reason to stand up.

My father asks me every morning what my plan is for the day and I stare at him. I don't have a plan. I don't have a thought. It's like my head is full of wet sand and nothing moves through it fast enough to become an intention.

At the family shop I sit behind the counter and customers come and go but I can't make myself greet them. My uncle gets angry and says I'm killing business, but it's not attitude — my mouth won't form the words, like speaking takes a kind of fuel I've run out of.

My wife makes dinner and I eat it without tasting anything. She asks if the salt is right and I say yes without knowing. It's not that I don't care — it's that everything reaches me through something thick, like I'm experiencing my own life from behind a wall.

I was supposed to visit my grandmother in hospital last week. I got dressed, put on my shoes, and then sat on the bed for three hours. My brother came home and found me still there. I couldn't explain what stopped me because nothing did — I just didn't move.

During Lunar New Year my cousins were playing cards and my aunt kept pulling me to join. I sat at the table but my hands felt too heavy to hold the cards. Everyone thought I was sulking but my body felt like it was made of clay — dense and useless.

My manager assigned me a simple report two weeks ago. The file is open on my screen right now. I read the first line every morning and then my mind goes blank — not distracted, just empty, like a room with the lights off.

I've worn the same clothes for five days. My roommate noticed and asked if I was okay. I know I should shower but the bathroom feels impossibly far — three meters away and it might as well be another city.

My mother calls every evening and I let it ring. Afterward I stare at the missed call and feel something distant, like guilt through a fog. But calling back means talking, and talking means finding energy I haven't had in weeks.

At the neighbourhood temple gathering I sat in the back and couldn't follow the chanting. My lips wouldn't move. The woman next to me asked if I was feeling well and I said yes because it's easier than explaining that I feel like something unplugged me from the inside.

I sleep maybe four hours a night and my body never feels heavy enough to rest. Even when my mother turns off all the lights and tells me to lie down after lunch, I'm up within minutes — my legs won't stay still and something in my chest keeps pushing me to move.

Everyone in my family naps after the big Sunday meal — my father on the sofa, my brother on the floor mat. I'm the only one pacing the kitchen, cleaning things that are already clean, because sitting in that post-meal stillness makes me feel like I'm suffocating.

My grandmother says I have no patience for the earth — that I need to sit and let things grow. But I can't. At harvest festival when everyone rests after the cooking, I'm already planning the next task. My body doesn't know how to be finished.

After my surgery the doctor said complete bed rest for two weeks. By day three I was reorganizing the cupboards from my bed, directing the household help, answering work calls. My husband hid my phone and I felt like he'd taken my oxygen.

I take the earliest train to the office and the last one home. My colleagues ask when I rest and I don't have an answer. Even on the train I'm reading reports — the thought of staring out the window at nothing makes me feel like I'm losing ground.

My mother-in-law keeps telling me to eat slowly, sit after meals, stop rushing. But when I try to sit with her after dinner, my mind races so fast I can't hear what she's saying. I need to be doing something or I feel like I'll shake apart.

During the long holiday when the whole joint family gathers and everyone goes slow — late breakfasts, afternoon naps, evening walks — I feel like I'm vibrating at a different frequency. I can't match their rhythm. By day two I've picked a fight with my cousin just to discharge the restlessness.

My friend says I look thinner every time she sees me. I forget to eat because stopping to cook means stopping to be still, and stillness feels like a pit I'll fall into. So I keep moving and grab biscuits from the jar when my hands shake.

At night when the neighbourhood goes quiet and my children are asleep, I should feel peace but instead I feel exposed. The silence has a weight I can't bear — I turn on the TV or start folding laundry just to keep something between me and that empty stillness.

My team at work respects me because I never stop. But last month I fainted at my desk and the office doctor said my body is running on nothing — no reserves, no recovery. I understood what he meant but I don't know how to build that back. Being still feels like a language I never learned.

I've started noticing there are about three distinct flavors my mornings come in — heavy and stuck, buzzy and scattered, or this quieter steadiness. When I catch which one showed up before I even open my laptop, the whole day goes differently because I stop fighting whatever weather is already running.

Mid-meeting I'll catch that the impatience making me interrupt people isn't about the agenda — it's a restless current that's been humming since I woke up. Just naming it as a mood-weather thing, not a content thing, loosens its grip enough that I stop derailing the conversation.

When I've been reaching for heavy takeout three nights running, I can usually trace it back — there's a dullness that settled in days ago that I mistook for tiredness. Once I see that pattern, I start choosing lighter food without forcing it, and within a day or two something lifts.

Even on a clear day when I'm making good decisions and feeling sharp, there's a part of me that registers — this clearness is also a passing quality, not bedrock. It's like noticing that the cleanest window is still glass between me and what's outside.

Before I say yes to weekend plans with friends, I check what's been running in me all week. If it's been scattered and wired, I know a loud bar will just amplify that. If I've been sluggish, I know staying home will make it worse. My social life basically negotiates around whatever tone is already set.

My wife notices before I do. She'll say something like 'you've gone flat' and I'll realize, oh — I slipped into that foggy gear two days ago and didn't register it. Now I treat her observations like a mirror I actually trust more than my own read.

I choose my gym routine based on what I wake up with. If there's a dull heaviness, I need something that gets my heart rate up fast. If I'm already wired, I go for slow stretching. It's not a fitness plan — it's me trying to shift whatever coloring showed up uninvited.

At my daughter's school pickup I can feel which mode I'm in by how I respond to other parents making small talk. Some days I'm genuinely warm and open. Other days everything they say irritates me. I've stopped blaming them — it's whatever lens dropped over me hours ago.

I protect Sunday mornings no matter what — not as a hobby but because that's where I recalibrate. If I skip it, by Tuesday I can't tell what's me and what's just the mood-weather running things. My partner knows not to schedule anything then.

Walking through the grocery store, I'll notice I'm drawn to junk food and realize the pull isn't hunger — it's a heaviness looking for company. That small recognition is enough to shift the cart toward something simpler. Not willpower, just seeing what's actually happening.

I've become so tuned to tracking my inner weather that I can't stop. I'm monitoring myself all day — am I heavy, am I wired, am I clear — and the monitoring itself has become exhausting. I can't just be in a mood anymore; every feeling gets sorted and labelled before I've even felt it.

My husband says I've become impossible to plan with. I'll commit to dinner with another couple and then cancel because I've decided my state isn't right for socializing. I know it's controlling, but going out when I'm in the wrong gear ruins it for everyone — or at least that's what I tell myself.

I reorganize my entire day based on whatever quality I detect in the first ten minutes of waking. If I sense heaviness, I cancel my afternoon calls. If I'm too activated, I won't drive. My calendar has become hostage to my self-reading and my boss is starting to notice.

At team lunches I'm silently diagnosing everyone — that guy's scattered, she's sluggish, he's in a clear patch. I can't stop classifying people's moods into these categories. It makes me feel like I see something others don't, but honestly it's isolating.

I've built this elaborate morning system — specific foods, specific light exposure, specific movement — all designed to guarantee I start the day in the right quality. If anything disrupts it, I spiral. My roommate bumped into me in the kitchen last Tuesday and I was rattled for hours because my sequence broke.

I judge my mother harshly when she seems stuck in a dull, heavy place. I want to shake her out of it because I can see so clearly what's happening. She just thinks I'm being critical. I know I'm projecting my framework onto her but I genuinely can't stop.
