I've started refusing invitations unless I can guarantee I'll be in the right inner state. Birthday parties, barbecues — I'll agree provisionally and then back out morning-of because I woke up foggy. My friends have mostly stopped asking.

Even my clear, steady moments get analyzed to death. I'll be feeling genuinely good and then I start worrying — is this real clarity or am I just in a manic upswing disguised as steadiness? I can't let any state just be what it is anymore.

I told my therapist I need to track these inner weather patterns or I'll lose myself. She pointed out that the tracking has become its own prison — that I'm now anxious about being anxious, heavy about noticing heaviness. She's probably right but I don't know how to stop.

At work I've become the person who says things like 'the room feels scattered today' and people look at me like I'm odd. I genuinely believe I'm sensing something real, but the way I organize my whole workday around it — rescheduling presentations because the energy feels wrong — is starting to affect my performance reviews.

I know I wake up in different moods but I just push through regardless. Coffee, commute, meetings — same routine no matter what. My girlfriend says I'm oblivious to what's running me, but honestly, who has time to sit around reading their own weather?

People talk about noticing shifts in their energy or mood quality throughout the day and I genuinely don't know what they mean. My days feel the same — I'm either fine or I'm not. There's nothing in between to notice.

My doctor asked if I notice patterns in when I reach for junk food versus when I eat well. I said no — I just eat what's there. She seemed to think that was important, like there was something I should be picking up on that I'm missing entirely.

I snapped at my kid this morning for no real reason, and my wife said 'you've been like this for days.' I hadn't noticed anything building. To me it felt sudden. Apparently everyone around me can see a shift that I can't detect in myself.

After work I'm either wired or completely flat, but I never think about why or what's been building toward that. I just deal with whatever hits me. The idea of catching it earlier in the day — like before it's fully set — never occurs to me in real time.

My buddy does this thing where he checks in with himself before agreeing to plans, and I think it's ridiculous. You're either up for things or you're not. I don't understand what there is to check. I just go and deal with however I feel when I get there.

I keep making the same bad decisions at the same times — overcommitting on Monday mornings when I feel invincible, then crashing by Wednesday. My manager pointed out the cycle months ago but I can't seem to catch it while it's happening. I only see it in hindsight.

My sister says I go through these heavy patches where I withdraw from everyone and don't return calls for a week. She asks me to notice when it's starting. But to me there's no starting — one day I just realize I haven't talked to anyone and I don't know when that began.

I picked a fight with my neighbor about his recycling bins and later realized I'd been irritable all day, maybe all week. I thought I was just responding to what was in front of me. The idea that something was already coloring everything before the bins — I can sort of see it now but I can't see it in the moment.

At the gym I just do the same workout regardless of how I feel walking in. My trainer keeps asking what my energy's like today and I never have an answer. It all just feels like 'body' to me — I don't know what she's distinguishing between.

I've started noticing what kind of quality is sitting in me before I even get out of bed. If there's a thickness, I know the chapatis will taste like nothing and I'll want to skip morning prayers. If there's a lightness, the whole house feels different. I adjust what I cook for everyone based on what I detect.

When my mother-in-law starts her usual criticisms at dinner, I used to react immediately. Now I catch that something heavy already settled over me the moment she entered the room — like a filter dropped. Knowing it's a filter, not a fact, gives me a breath before I respond.

On the bus to work, I can feel whether the day will be restless or sluggish or steady just from how my body sits. If I'm gripping the handle too tight and my mind is already in three meetings at once, I know what's coloring things and I try to eat something calming at my desk before the morning starts.

During festivals when the whole family gathers, I notice which quality takes over the house — the busyness has a frantic edge or a warm clarity depending on the day. I've learned to step out to the balcony and check what's running in me specifically, separate from the collective mood.

My father doesn't understand why I choose warm, light food some weeks and heavier dishes other weeks. I've noticed that what I eat either feeds whatever dullness or agitation is already there, or gently shifts it. He thinks I'm being fussy, but it's more like I'm adjusting the coloring from inside.

Even when everything at home is harmonious — good food, the children are calm, my husband is kind — there's a quiet part of me that notes: this peace is also a quality passing through. It doesn't make me sad. It just keeps me from gripping it too hard when it starts to change.

When I visit my grandmother and she's in one of her dark, withdrawn moods, I used to absorb it completely. Now I notice the heaviness landing on me like a cloth being laid over my chest, and I can name it as something that entered rather than something that is me. It still affects me, but less completely.

At the office, before a difficult conversation with my manager, I take a moment to check what's already coloring my perception. If I'm already in a restless, reactive state, I know I'll hear criticism where none is intended. That small pause has saved me from unnecessary conflicts.

I choose my morning activity based on what shows up — if my mind is foggy and my limbs feel like stone, I do vigorous cleaning. If I'm already wired from a dream or worry, I sit with my tea longer. My husband thinks I'm inconsistent, but I'm responding to whatever tone is already set.

Walking through the market, surrounded by noise and people, I can catch the moment a scattered, agitated quality takes over — suddenly every vendor's voice irritates me and I want to rush. Recognizing it as a shift in my inner weather rather than the market's fault lets me slow down and finish shopping without snapping at anyone.

I monitor every family member's mood-quality constantly — my daughter is sluggish, my husband is agitated, my mother seems clear today. I adjust the food, the conversation topics, everything, to try to shift everyone toward steadiness. It's exhausting and my sister says I treat the household like a project I need to manage.

I cannot attend a family gathering without spending the whole time reading the room — who's heavy, who's restless, who's genuinely light. I rearrange seating, change the music, redirect conversation. My cousins think I'm controlling. I think I'm the only one who can see what's actually happening.

I've built my entire cooking around correcting whatever quality I sense in the household each morning. Hot ginger if things feel sluggish, cooling foods if everyone seems agitated. My mother-in-law says I've become obsessive about food and she's probably right — I can't just cook simply anymore.

If I wake up and sense any heaviness at all, I will not leave the house. I've missed weddings, my nephew's birthday, work deadlines — all because I refuse to operate under what I consider the wrong inner condition. My family is losing patience with me.

At the office I've become rigid about when I'll take meetings. If I sense restlessness in myself I refuse calls because I know I'll say something sharp. My colleagues have started working around my unpredictable availability and my boss told me directly that it's affecting the team.

I spend so much time trying to stay in a clear, steady state that I've eliminated almost everything from my life — no late dinners, no spontaneous visits from neighbours, no rich food. My children say I've become rigid and joyless in the name of balance.

When my neighbour visits for tea, I'm silently categorizing her entire demeanor — she's heavy today, probably ate too late, her mind is dull. I catch myself wanting to correct her habits. She came for company and I'm mentally prescribing her a different life.

During prayer time I can't stop evaluating whether my inner state is pure enough for it to count. If I sense even a trace of restlessness or fog, I abandon the practice and try again later. Some days I never get to it at all because no moment feels clean enough.

I told my husband I can see three distinct veils that cover our experience and he looked at me like I was losing it. I've started reorganizing our entire routine — sleep times, meal times, social obligations — around what I detect. He says I've replaced one kind of rigidity with another.

I judge my in-laws constantly for what I perceive as their unawareness. They eat heavy food at night, stay up watching television, and then wonder why mornings are difficult. I feel superior for seeing the pattern, but this superiority has made family dinners tense and lonely.

My wife says I've been irritable for a week but I can't see what she's talking about. I feel normal. The children avoid me at dinner and I only notice because the chair beside me stays empty. Whatever shift happened, I missed when it started.

I eat whatever my mother puts in front of me without thinking. Rice at night, fried snacks in the afternoon — it never occurs to me that what I eat might be connected to why I feel like a stone by evening. When she asks how I feel, I say 'fine' because I genuinely don't distinguish between states.

My colleague mentioned that I seem to have a cycle — energetic at the start of the week then completely withdrawn by Thursday. I hadn't noticed any cycle. Each day just arrives and I deal with whatever's in front of me without checking what's running underneath.

My grandmother keeps telling me to notice what food does to my mind, how sleep changes my temperament, how certain company makes me dull. I nod politely but honestly, I don't register differences. I'm either okay or I'm tired — there's nothing finer to detect.

After family pujas I feel nothing different. Everyone talks about feeling lighter or more settled afterward and I just feel the same as before. My aunt says I'm not paying attention to what shifts inside, but there's nothing there for me to notice.

I said something harsh to my younger sister this morning and she cried. My mother said I've had this dark cloud over me for days. I thought I was being normal. Whatever everyone else is seeing, I'm not detecting it from inside — it just feels like regular me.

On festival days the whole family seems to shift into a brighter, warmer state. I feel the same as any other day. I participate in the rituals, eat the food, wear the clothes — but whatever inner change others report, it doesn't seem to reach me or I can't tell the difference.

My manager asked why I always schedule difficult client calls at four p.m. when I'm clearly at my lowest energy. I didn't realize I had a lowest energy time. She says it's obvious to everyone — I get slow and vague after lunch — but from inside, the afternoon feels the same as the morning to me.

My husband meditates every morning and adjusts his whole day around what he notices. I've tried sitting quietly and I genuinely detect nothing — no qualities, no flavors, no tones. Just silence and then my to-do list. He says everyone has these shifts; I think some people just don't.

My neighbour brought over heavy sweets last night and I ate them without thinking. This morning my body is like clay and my mind won't start. If someone asked, did I notice anything shift after eating? No — I only connect it now because my daughter pointed out that I always feel terrible after those sweets. I never catch it in the moment.

When I'm walking my dog in the morning and the light hits the frost a certain way, I feel two things at once — this sharp love for the specific glitter on the grass, and something behind it that has no shape or color at all. I don't try to pick one. Both run the whole walk.

My wife is talking about her day — what her boss said, how the copier jammed — and I'm genuinely tracking every detail. But underneath all of it there's this quiet that doesn't belong to either of us or anything she's describing. I hold both without choosing.

At work during a brainstorm, I'm fully in it — someone's whiteboard sketch, the marker smell, the energy in the room. And at the same time there's this sense that the whole scene is resting on something that has nothing to do with ideas or people. I operate from both places simultaneously.

When I hold my son after his bath, his warm little body and the soap smell are completely real and important to me. But I also notice something that's not him, not me, not the room — something that doesn't need any of this to exist. I give weight to both.

I'm at a dinner party, genuinely enjoying the wine, the conversation, the particular way my friend laughs. None of it feels less real because I also sense something behind all of it that has no personality, no warmth, no edges. I just let both registers play.

My body responds to things — cold morning air on my arms, the weight of a blanket at night — and I take that seriously, it matters. But I also notice that whatever registers all of this isn't itself cold or warm or heavy. I used to think I had to pick one as more real, but now I just live with both running at once.

During my commute, the traffic, the radio, the red lights — all that specific chaos is fully there. But so is this backdrop that couldn't care less about any of it. I don't dismiss the traffic or cling to the silence. Both organize how I move through the morning.

When I visit my parents and my mom fusses over dinner plates, I'm completely present to her — her hands, her voice, the clinking. And there's also this vast nothing-in-particular behind the whole kitchen scene. I don't need to resolve it. Both are just there, equally solid.

Standing in line at the grocery store, watching the cashier scan items, the beeps, the fluorescent light — it's all specific and unremarkable. But running underneath is this sense of something that has no location, no features. I relate to both without drama.

When I transition from work to picking up my kids, there's a shift in roles — but beneath both roles there's something that doesn't shift at all. I honor the role and the no-role equally. Neither one wins.

I've gotten to where every single thing — my coworker's coffee mug, the pattern on the carpet — I'm searching it for that formless thing behind it. I can't just enjoy a sunset anymore without mentally peeling it apart. My girlfriend says I look through her instead of at her.

At my nephew's birthday party, I could barely engage because I kept feeling like the balloons, the cake, the screaming kids were all just a thin screen over something else. I know that's not fair to him. But I couldn't stop looking past everything.

My partner says I treat ordinary moments like they're tests — like I'm always checking whether I can detect the formless behind the form. She says it makes her feel like she's never enough on her own terms. I don't know how to stop doing it.

I spend so much energy tracking both layers — the visible stuff and the nothing underneath — that I'm exhausted by lunch. My body is tense from holding two frequencies at once. I can't just be in one place anymore.

In meetings I get frustrated because everyone is only talking about the surface — the numbers, the deadlines — and no one seems to notice there's something else behind all of it. I feel alienated. I can't just stay at the level they're at.
Even when I'm making love, part of me is reaching past my wife's body toward something impersonal. She noticed. She asked if I was even with her. I was — and I wasn't. It's becoming a problem.

I judge people who seem to only live in the visible world — my brother, my coworkers. Like they're missing half of what's here. I know it's arrogant but I can't help it. Everything they care about seems incomplete to me.

I've started neglecting practical things — bills, dentist appointments — because they feel like they belong only to the surface layer and I'm increasingly drawn to what's behind all of it. My roommate had to remind me rent was overdue twice.

When my friend was crying about her divorce, I felt compassion but also this pull to tell her that none of it is ultimately real — that there's something behind all the hurt that's untouched. I didn't say it, but I wanted to, and that scares me.

I can't enjoy a walk in the park without it becoming a two-track experience. The trees, the joggers — all of it is immediately also this other thing. It's like I've lost the ability to just be simply here. Everything is always double.
I just see what's in front of me — my desk, my lunch, my coworkers — and that's it. If there's something behind all of it, I've never felt it. My world is just the stuff that's here.

My sister talks about sensing something beyond the everyday and I honestly don't know what she means. For me, the everyday is the whole story. I sometimes wonder if I'm missing a channel other people have.
When I look at my kids, I see my kids. Full stop. I love them, I worry about them, but there's no second layer I detect. If something formless is there, I'm apparently blind to it.
At funerals I feel grief — raw, physical grief. The loss is total and concrete. I don't have some backup sense of something beyond the person who's gone. There's just the absence.

In quiet moments — early morning, late night — other people seem to access some deeper register. I just hear the fridge hum. I'm not tuned to anything beyond the surfaces of things, and honestly, I'm not sure there's anything to tune into.
My therapist asks if I sense anything beyond my thoughts and emotions and body. I don't. Those three things are my whole world. If there's a fourth, it's not showing up for me.
When I'm stressed at work, the stress is all there is. There's no calm backdrop I can fall into. Everything is just the surface — the email, the deadline, my tight shoulders — and nothing underneath.

My neighbor invited me to her silent retreat group and I went once. Everyone else seemed to contact something beyond the room. I just sat there feeling my butt go numb on the cushion. The concrete world is the only one I access.

I used to think maybe there was something behind all the stuff of life, but honestly I've given up looking. The forms — people, objects, weather — they're it for me. That's my whole reality and nothing else opens up.

After my father died, people told me he's still here in some way. I wanted to feel that. But all I feel is the empty chair, the missing voice. The visible world is absolute to me — when something leaves it, it's gone.

When I do the morning pooja, I'm fully with the flame, the kumkum, the brass — each thing is alive and specific. But behind my closed eyes there's also this emptiness that isn't sad, just boundless. I give the same respect to both. Neither one is a stepping stone to the other.

Feeding my grandmother her afternoon rice, I feel the weight of the spoon, her trembling jaw, the ceiling fan — all this vivid detail. And also something that has no age, no body, no name, holding the whole scene. I serve her knowing both are real.

At the office, my manager is discussing quarterly targets and I'm fully attentive — the spreadsheet, his specific gestures. But there's also this space behind the whole room that has no interest in numbers. I function from both without confusion.

During a festival, the noise, the colors, the sweets passed hand to hand — I enjoy every specific thing. And simultaneously there's a silence that doesn't compete with any of it. Both run through me at the same time, and neither wins over the other.

My mother-in-law is particular about how I fold the saris, and I pay real attention to the silk and the creases. But even while my hands are precise, there's something behind the whole activity — formless, indifferent to silk — and I rest there too.

Riding the metro home, the crowd is pressing and specific — someone's sweat, someone's bag against my arm. I'm fully here for that. And also aware that whatever contains all this contact has no skin, no edge. I don't need to choose between the crowd and the spacelessness.

When my father gives advice at dinner — the way his voice goes firm, the clink of his glass — I honor all that form, his specific authority. And I also feel something behind him that has nothing to do with being a father or a man. I receive both without ranking them.

Watching the neighbor's children play in the corridor, their shouts and their scraped knees — it's all completely real and I care. But the corridor also rests on something that doesn't know noise or skin. I notice both with the same steadiness.

When I wake before dawn and the house is still dark, my body is warm and specific under the quilt. But the darkness itself seems to hold something that has no temperature, no weight. I don't need to move toward one and away from the other. Both feel like home.

My colleague at the next desk is eating her lunch — the smell of sambar, the sound of her spoon. It's vivid and particular. And behind all that specificity, something is perfectly blank and still. I operate within both and my workday runs fine.

I've started seeing the deities at the temple not as themselves but only as pointers to the formless. I can't bow to the murti anymore with real feeling — I'm impatient with it, like it's just a costume. My mother noticed I rush through the aarti now.

My wife complains I've become distant. When she talks about the children's school fees or what to cook, I feel like those things are just surface noise. I'm always pulled toward the nothing behind it all, and the daily stuff feels like an interruption.

At family gatherings I sit apart now. Everyone is laughing, eating, arguing — and I feel irritated because they seem trapped in just the visible layer. I can't pretend that layer is enough anymore. My uncle asked if something is wrong with me.

I used to enjoy cooking elaborate meals. Now I resent the time it takes because the flavors and textures feel like they're pulling me away from something deeper. I've started simplifying everything — plain rice, plain dal — and my family doesn't understand why.

When my neighbor brings sweets during Diwali, I go through the motions of receiving them, but inside I feel almost angry that people invest so much in these forms — the box, the wrapping, the specific gulab jamun. It feels like everyone is ignoring what actually matters.

My manager praised my presentation and I felt nothing. Not humble, not grateful — just this sense that the whole professional world is like a puppet show over an empty stage. I'm losing the ability to engage with the puppets.

I correct my children when they get excited about new toys or movies — telling them these things don't last, that something else is more important. My husband says I'm stealing their childhood. Maybe I am. But I can't stop pointing them past the forms.

Even during my parents' anniversary, I couldn't stay present to the celebration. The flowers, the garlands, the photography — it all felt false. I kept wanting everyone to stop and acknowledge that behind all this show there's something else entirely.

I've been sleeping poorly because lying in bed, my body feels like just a shell — and I'm restless trying to reach past it. I don't want to be in the form anymore. My wife says I toss and mutter all night.

My friend invited me to a music concert and I went, but the ragas, the tabla — all of it felt like a distraction from the silence. I wanted to stand up and walk out. I can't enjoy anything that has shape or sound anymore without feeling it's pulling me away from what's real.

For me the world is fully solid — the food on the table, my daughter's homework, the traffic. If something beyond all this exists, I have no way to feel it. Everything begins and ends with what I can touch.

My mother prays every morning and says she feels something beyond the lamp and the flowers. When I sit beside her, I just feel the hard floor under my knees. Whatever she's reaching, I don't reach it. The objects are all I get.

At the temple, the crowd pushes forward for darshan and everyone seems to see something in the deity's face. I see painted stone. I love the ritual — the bells, the camphor — but they're just bells and camphor to me. No second layer opens.
When my grandfather died, people said his essence remains. All I feel is the empty cot, the smell of his room fading. What's gone is gone. I can't sense anything left behind that isn't physical.

My colleague meditates during lunch break and says she touches something formless. I tried sitting with her once. I just heard the AC unit and felt my stomach growling. The world stays resolutely concrete for me.

During festivals, I enjoy the sweets, the clothes, the visiting — but when my aunt says these celebrations point to something invisible, I don't feel it. For me the celebration is the thing itself. There's no behind.

My wife lights the evening lamp and says the flame represents something beyond itself. I see a wick burning oil. I love her devotion, but whatever she contacts through the flame, I'm locked out of it. My world is only what's visible.

When I'm stuck in traffic after work, exhausted, honking — there's nothing underneath it. No deeper quiet, no backdrop. Just the noise, the fumes, my aching back. If there's a formless dimension behind this city, my body has never registered it.

My neighbor talks about how nature reveals something beyond itself — the banyan tree, the river at dawn. When I look at the banyan, I see bark and roots. Beautiful, yes, but only bark and roots. Nothing behind them opens for me.

After my son was born, everyone said I must feel the presence of something greater. I felt his weight, his cry, the hospital smell. Real and wonderful — but fully contained in what's here. No formless layer showed up. Just a baby, breathing in my arms.

trigunas\trajas-projects-tamas-veils-sat.display_name: Rajas Projects, Tamas Veils, Sattva Reveals

statements.statement: I notice that when I'm rushing around reorganizing my desk or answering emails I don't need to answer, I'm not actually solving anything — I'm building a busy picture over something I don't want to feel. It's only when I finally stop, maybe sitting in the car after work, that the real thing underneath shows itself, usually something embarrassingly simple like loneliness.

There's a pattern where I go weeks genuinely unable to see that something is wrong between me and my wife. It's not that I'm avoiding it — the whole topic is just blank, like it doesn't exist. Then one quiet Sunday morning it surfaces clearly and I think, how was I walking around not knowing this?

After a few days of stillness — no big plans, no podcasts, just ordinary mornings — I start to see things about my life that were apparently there all along. Like realizing I've been resentful toward my neighbor for months without registering it as resentment until the noise in me died down.

I've learned that my clarity about people comes only after the excitement or the fog lifts. When I first meet someone, I either spin stories about who they are or I can't read them at all. The accurate sense only arrives later, in a settled moment, like it was waiting for the interference to clear.

At team meetings, when everyone is brainstorming fast, I notice my mind painting elaborate scenarios that feel brilliant in the moment. An hour later, sitting alone with my coffee, I can tell that most of it was just momentum — the real insight is smaller and quieter and was hiding under all that energy.

I protect my Saturday mornings — no phone, no plans. My partner used to think it was antisocial, but it's the only window where I can actually see what's going on with me. Without it, the rest of the week is either frantic activity or a kind of numbness where I can't tell what I feel.

When I'm walking my dog at dawn before the day starts, I sometimes see my own situation with total plainness — like, oh, I'm afraid of my boss, that's what's been running this whole month. It's never dramatic. It's just there, obvious, once the churning and the fog both thin out.

I can tell whether I'm seeing my kids clearly by how still my mind is. If I've been in work mode all day — spinning, planning, pitching — I come home and project things onto them that aren't there. If I've had a quiet drive home with no music, I can actually see what they need.

There are three modes I cycle through with my friend group: sometimes I genuinely can't register that there's tension, sometimes I'm manufacturing drama where there isn't any, and occasionally I just see what's actually happening between us. The third one only shows up when I'm rested and unrushed.

At a neighborhood barbecue I catch myself mid-conversation constructing a whole story about why someone said what they said — motive, history, subtext — and I know from experience that none of it is real. The true read, if I get one, comes the next day when the social buzz has settled and something plain and small rises up.

I cannot stop planning my response to my manager before she even finishes talking. The version of the conversation I build in my head is so vivid it feels more real than what's actually happening in the room. By the time I get home I realize I responded to something she never actually said.

When my brother mentions our inheritance, my mind immediately spins a whole future — scenarios, confrontations, betrayals — and I'm living inside that fiction all evening. My wife says I get tense and distant but to me it feels like I'm solving something urgent.

I keep redesigning my budget spreadsheet, my workout plan, my morning routine — always tweaking the system. My girlfriend says I'm not actually living, I'm building a movie set of a life. She might be right but the building feels productive while I'm doing it.

At parties I find myself narrating everything internally — who's with whom, what that glance meant, what they think of me. It's like a constant second layer being painted over the actual room. I leave exhausted from a reality I mostly invented.

Every time my mother calls, within thirty seconds I've already constructed what she's really saying underneath her words. I'm so busy interpreting that I miss her actual tone. Later my sister tells me Mom sounded fine and I wonder what conversation I was having.

I woke up at 3 a.m. replaying a conversation with a coworker, but the version in my head had become this whole dramatic betrayal narrative. My chest was tight, my jaw clenched. By morning I knew none of it had actually happened that way, but my body had lived through it as if it did.

When I'm in a creative surge at work, everything looks like opportunity and connection — patterns everywhere, meaning in every coincidence. My colleague says I'm spinning stories. He's probably right, because when I come down from it a few days later, the connections dissolve and the plain facts look much simpler.

I project so much onto new people I date that by the third date I've already built an entire relationship in my mind. It's vivid — I can see our apartment, our arguments, our future. The actual person in front of me keeps not matching, and I feel confused about who's wrong.

On the subway I catch myself writing whole backstories for strangers — that man is unhappy in his marriage, that woman just got a promotion. It happens automatically and feels like perception, but I know it's fabrication because occasionally someone speaks and shatters the image completely.

I'm driving to my parents' house for Thanksgiving and the whole way there I'm scripting confrontations, rehearsing defenses, feeling my pulse rise at things nobody has said yet. By the time I arrive, I'm already angry at a dinner that hasn't happened.

I've been at the same job for two years and I genuinely cannot tell if I like it or not. It's not that I haven't thought about it — it's more like the question slides away every time I approach it. My therapist asks what I feel about work and I just go blank.

My best friend told me she'd been worried about me for months and I had no idea she felt that way — or that there was anything to worry about. It's like my own situation was covered over by something I couldn't see through, and I still can't fully see it even now that she's pointed at it.

I sleep ten hours and still feel like something thick is between me and the day. Not sadness exactly — more like a blanket over everything that keeps me from registering what's actually going on around me. My roommate talks and I hear the words but nothing lands.

At family dinners I sit there and I know something is off between my parents but I can't name it or feel it properly. It's not that I'm choosing not to engage — it's like the channel that would let me pick up on it is just turned off.

I keep missing obvious things about my own health — I didn't notice I'd lost weight until my jeans literally fell off, didn't notice the headaches were daily until my doctor asked. It's like there's a fog between me and my own body that only lifts when someone else points something out.

People at work will say something like 'that meeting was really tense, did you notice?' and I genuinely didn't. I was there, I heard everything, but the tension didn't register. It's as if the emotional layer of what's happening gets hidden from me by default.

My partner says I go through phases where I'm 'just not here' — not distracted with something else, just absent. Like I'm looking at our life through frosted glass. I can't argue because when those phases lift, I realize how much I'd been missing.

When my neighbor moved away, everyone at the going-away party was emotional and I felt nothing — not coldness, just blankness. A month later, doing dishes one night, the sadness finally appeared and I was shocked by how much was there. I don't know where it was hiding.

I realize I haven't had a real opinion about anything in weeks — what to eat, where to go, whether I'm happy. The questions float past me like they're addressed to someone else. It's not peace; it's more like being wrapped in cotton where nothing gets through.

My college friends plan a trip every year and I always say yes without checking in with myself at all. It's only mid-trip that I sometimes surface enough to notice I didn't actually want to come — but by then I can't remember what wanting or not-wanting even felt like when I said yes.

When my mother-in-law is fussing over lunch preparations, I sometimes catch myself building an inner story — she's criticizing how I keep my kitchen, she disapproves of me. But if I sit with the dal cooling in front of me and just breathe, I see that she's only showing affection the way she knows how, and my story dissolves.

During festival season when the house is full of relatives and noise, I notice I can't tell what I actually feel about any of it — it's all buried under the activity. It's only after everyone leaves and I'm folding the extra bedsheets that something simple and true surfaces, like relief or gratitude, clear as a bell.

I ride the crowded morning train to work and my mind manufactures dramas about strangers — that uncle is judging my clothes, that woman thinks I'm taking too much space. When I catch a seat and close my eyes for a few stops, the fabrications fall away and I realize nobody was paying attention to me at all.

My father gives advice at dinner every night and for years I couldn't see clearly whether he was controlling or caring. Either I'd spin into anger about it or I'd go numb and not register it at all. Now, on quiet mornings before anyone wakes up, I can hold both without the distortion — he's anxious and he loves me. That clarity only comes when I'm still.

After the morning puja my grandmother does, the house has a quality where I can think straight — like the mental noise has a gap in it. It's not mystical, it's just that in those twenty minutes I can actually see what's bothering me instead of spinning stories or going blank about it.

When my neighbor aunty comes to gossip about other families, I notice two things happening in me: either I get excited and start building whole narratives about people I barely know, or some part of me goes dull and I stop caring. The few times I just listen without either reaction, I can tell what's actually true in what she's saying and what isn't.

My manager calls a meeting and my mind races ahead — what he wants, what I should say, what the politics are. It's vivid but invented. Only after lunch, eating alone in the canteen, do I see what actually happened: he just needed a status update and there was nothing behind it.

I notice that when I'm cooking rice in the evening and the kitchen is quiet, I can suddenly see something about my marriage that's been there for weeks — a small hurt, a missed bid. During the busy day it's either invisible or it gets exaggerated into something dramatic. The truth is always smaller and simpler than what my active mind makes of it.

Walking back from the temple on a still evening, I sometimes see my own life very plainly — what I'm avoiding, what I actually want. It lasts maybe five minutes before the phone buzzes or someone calls my name and the clarity gets replaced by the usual hum of plans and worries. But those five minutes are what keep me oriented.

During family gatherings, if I've rested well and haven't been on my phone, I can read the room accurately — who's upset, who's pretending, what's unspoken. If I arrive already agitated from the commute, I project meanings onto everyone that turn out to be wrong. The difference isn't intelligence, it's some kind of inner quiet that either is or isn't there.

Before every family wedding I build an entire mental drama — who will say what about my weight, what my aunt will imply about my marriage, how I'll respond. My body tenses for days beforehand. At the actual event, none of it happens the way I imagined, but by then I've already lived through a wedding that was mostly my own creation.

When my mother mentions someone's son getting promoted, my mind instantly spins a whole comparison — his life versus mine, his mother's pride versus my mother's disappointment. It's so fast and vivid I feel ashamed before anyone has actually said anything critical. The shame is real but the insult never happened.

At the office I interpret every small gesture from my senior — a short email, a meeting I wasn't copied on — as evidence of a larger story about my standing. I build this elaborate architecture of office politics in my head and act from inside it. My colleague says I'm imagining things. Usually she's right.

Every time my in-laws visit, I'm already narrating their judgments before they've removed their shoes — what they'll think of the curtains, the food, my children's manners. I'm tense and defensive all through dinner, reacting to a script running in my own head while they're just eating and chatting normally.

I cannot stop mentally rehearsing conversations with my father about my career choice. The versions I build are so detailed — his tone, his pauses, my mother intervening — that when we actually sit down to talk, I'm confused because reality doesn't match any of my rehearsals.

On the bus home from work, if someone glances at me I instantly generate a whole story about what they must be thinking. It's reflexive and it colors the rest of my evening. My husband says I come home wound up about nothing, and he's probably right — I'm living inside pictures I painted over ordinary strangers.

When my team lead assigns a project to someone else, I spin for hours — it means he doesn't trust me, I'm being sidelined, I should start looking for another position. The projection is so real it keeps me up at night. By morning, if I'm lucky, I see it was just workload balancing and nothing more.

I visit my grandparents' house and before I've even entered, I'm constructing what they'll ask about marriage and what my uncle will imply. My chest is already tight on the doorstep. The evening becomes a battle with imaginary opponents while real people are just passing me sweets and asking about my commute.

My neighbor mentions her daughter's exam results and immediately I've built a whole comparative narrative — my child versus hers, my parenting versus hers, our futures. I feel competitive and agitated for the rest of the afternoon about a contest nobody else is having.

At the monthly department lunch, every joke, every seating arrangement, every eye contact becomes evidence for a political narrative I'm constructing. I eat quickly and leave with a stomach ache, having spent the whole meal inside a story that probably has very little to do with what actually happened at that table.

My mother has been ill for weeks and I know this but somehow it doesn't register properly — I go to work, I come home, I eat dinner she prepared, and the seriousness of it just doesn't land. It's as though there's a thick layer between me and the reality of her condition that I cannot push through on my own.

Everyone at the office seems to sense when the boss is in a bad mood and they adjust accordingly. I never pick up on it until someone tells me directly. It's not that I don't care — it's like that channel is blocked for me and I'm walking through my day unable to see what's obvious to everyone else.

My wife says I've been distant for months and I genuinely don't know what she means. When she describes specific moments — me not responding, not noticing her tears — I believe her, but I have no memory of those moments being significant. It's like a blanket was thrown over my perception at the time.

I can eat my grandmother's food every day and not really taste it or register the care behind it until someone visiting remarks on it and something briefly lifts. Then I see it clearly for a moment — all the effort, the love — before the thick ordinary dullness settles back over everything.

At family functions I'm physically present but something in me is covered over — people talk to me and I respond correctly, but I can't access what I actually feel about any of them until hours later, sometimes days, when something suddenly pierces through and I realize how much I've been missing.

My friend pointed out that I never noticed when our regular tea-stall uncle stopped showing up. Apparently he'd been gone two weeks. I pass that corner every day and the absence simply didn't register — like my perception had a hole where that awareness should be.

I sit through meetings where important decisions about my future are being made and I can't seem to feel that they matter. It's not that I've accepted whatever comes — it's more that the significance is hidden from me by something heavy and inert that I can't name or move.

My sister had her baby three months ago and I know I should feel something big — joy, tenderness, something. I hold the baby, I smile, but the real feeling is sealed away somewhere I can't reach. My mother noticed and asked if something was wrong. I couldn't even give her an honest answer because I don't know what's blocking it.

Every evening I come home, take off my shoes at the door, eat whatever has been made, watch my phone until sleep — and none of it leaves an impression. It's not routine comfort; it's more like I'm wrapped in something that prevents the texture of my life from reaching me. My father asks what happened today and I say 'nothing' because that's genuinely how it felt.

At the neighborhood temple during evening prayers, everyone around me seems moved or at least present, and I'm standing there with nothing — no feeling, no thought, just blankness. It's not doubt or rebellion. It's like the capacity to register meaning has been covered over by something dense that I can't lift by trying.

I can feel the exact moment in my morning when whatever film was over my mind lifts — usually after my walk, before I check email. That window is short but I've learned my whole day pivots on whether I protect it or let someone's text pull me out of it early.

When I cook a real dinner instead of ordering in, there's a lightness in my chest afterward that lasts into the next morning. It's not just nutrition — it's like the choice itself clears something that was sitting on me all day.

I've started noticing that the first ten minutes after I get home from work set the rest of the evening. If I drop onto the couch and scroll, I'm done — foggy and irritable with my wife by eight. If I change clothes and take five minutes outside first, something resets.

In meetings I can now feel when I shift from actually listening to performing — there's a buzzing, competitive edge that comes in, and my voice gets faster. I don't always stop it, but I see it, and that seeing gives me a tiny gap before I say something I'll regret.

I plan my weekends around what texture I need. After a high-pressure week I can't do brunch with loud friends — not because I don't love them, but because I know my system is already revved and I need the opposite. My partner thinks I'm being antisocial but it's survival.

When my mom calls and starts worrying aloud about everything, I can feel a heavy, sluggish coat settle over me within minutes. I've learned to take those calls on a walk, standing up, because if I'm already lying down I'll be useless the rest of the afternoon.

I pick my gym time carefully — early morning works because the restless push that builds later in the day hasn't started yet. If I wait until after work, I'm already wired and the workout just cranks me higher instead of centering me.

At my neighbor's barbecue last weekend I noticed myself cycling through three textures in two hours — calm and genuinely present, then jumpy and talking too fast, then dull and wanting to leave. I could almost watch the transitions happen like weather moving through.

I eat the same breakfast every day not because I love it but because I've tested what happens when I don't. A sugary pastry gives me a spike and then a fog by ten a.m. that affects my patience with my students. The oatmeal keeps the lens clear.

When I sit with my daughter doing homework and my mind is clear, I can feel the difference — I'm patient, I'm actually curious about her thinking. But if I've been doomscrolling for an hour before, there's this agitated film over everything and I catch myself snapping at her for going slow.

I track everything — my heart rate, my sleep score, what I ate, how many steps — and I'm adjusting constantly, all day, trying to stay in that one narrow band where I feel sharp. But the tracking itself has become another source of pressure, another thing buzzing in the background that won't let me rest.

I've become so sensitive to what pulls me off-center that I've basically eliminated anything unpredictable from my life. No spontaneous dinners, no unplanned conversations. My friend said I've become impossible to be around and honestly she's probably right, but the alternative is chaos.

I can't stop analyzing my own state. I'm in a conversation with my partner and half my attention is watching myself — am I calm? Am I reactive? Am I dull? The monitoring never stops and it's exhausting, like I'm living two inches behind my own life.

I judge everyone now by their energy — my coworker who eats fast food, my brother who watches TV all night. I feel their heaviness on me within minutes, and I've started pulling away from people I used to love because I can't tolerate being dragged into their fog.

Every choice feels loaded — what podcast I listen to on the commute, whether I check the news, which route I take. It's like I'm managing a fragile instrument all day and one wrong input ruins the whole calibration. My wife says I've made daily life into a full-time job.

I got into an argument with my roommate because she left the TV on all evening and I told her it was polluting the apartment. I know that sounds extreme but I physically felt something thicken in the air and by bedtime my mind was sluggish in a way it hadn't been all day.

At work I can feel the exact moment my manager's anxious energy enters my system — my shoulders tighten, my thoughts speed up. I've started leaving the room the second I notice it, which makes me look rude, but I'm terrified of losing whatever steadiness I built that morning.

I refuse to eat at restaurants my friends choose because I can predict what it'll do to me for the next six hours. Last week I brought my own food to someone's birthday dinner. The table went quiet and I know I crossed a line but I couldn't tolerate the alternative.

I'm so vigilant about not falling into dullness that I over-schedule myself — podcasts, cold showers, timed work sprints — and now I'm jittery and wired all the time. I swapped one problem for another and I can feel it in my racing pulse at midnight.

I've built this elaborate morning routine — two hours minimum — and if anything interrupts it I'm a wreck. My kids have learned not to talk to me before eight. I know that's messed up, but without it I feel like I'm walking into the day blind.

I eat whatever's fast, skip meals, go to bed at random hours, and I feel basically the same all the time — flat, gray, slightly annoyed. My girlfriend keeps saying small things would help but I can't see the connection between what I had for lunch and how I feel at six p.m.

People talk about feeling sharp in the morning or tired after certain foods and I honestly have no idea what they mean. My days are all one texture — slightly foggy, slightly irritable — and I've just accepted that's how I am.

My therapist asked what activities make me feel clear versus heavy and I drew a blank. I hadn't ever thought about it. Everything just happens to me and then I react. There's no pausing, no noticing — just the next thing and the next thing.

I binge-watch shows until two a.m. and then wonder why I can't think straight at work. It never occurs to me in the moment that one thing leads to the other — my friend had to point it out and even then I shrugged because it feels like that's just how evenings go.

My buddy says he can feel the difference between a good day and a bad day starting from how he slept and what he ate, and it sounds like superstition to me. I just push through whatever I'm feeling. There's no read-out — I'm blind to it.

At family dinners I'm the same whether it's a quiet meal with my sister or chaos with my in-laws — checked out, vaguely annoyed, waiting for it to end. My wife says I seem like I'm never really there and she's right, but I don't know what 'there' would feel like.

I drink coffee all day, energy drinks by two, and I'm still operating in this thick middle zone — not alert, not exhausted, just going. I couldn't tell you what clarity feels like versus dullness because the needle never moves enough for me to notice.

My coworkers talk about needing to decompress after certain clients and I don't get it. I feel the same whether the meeting was tense or relaxed. Not good, not bad — just nothing. I think something's supposed to register that doesn't.

On weekends I do whatever comes up — drive-through for breakfast, a movie marathon, maybe a beer by noon — and by Sunday night I feel depleted without understanding how. There's no sense that my choices during the day did something to me; it's like the fatigue just appeared from nowhere.

I've been told I should notice what drains me versus what restores me, but honestly both feel like the same flat line. I go through my day on autopilot — the gym, the commute, the meetings — and none of it registers as having a quality. It's all just stuff that happened.

I make the dal from scratch on Sundays — soaking the lentils the night before, tempering the spices slowly — and my whole family seems lighter at the table afterward. I notice that when I take that care, even my tone with my mother-in-law stays patient into the evening. The effort of cooking well isn't separate from the effort of being well.

After morning pooja I have maybe thirty minutes where my mind is still and my body feels settled — warm, quiet, no rush. I've learned to use that window for the hardest conversation of the day, because once the office WhatsApp groups start pinging, something shifts and I'm in a different mode entirely.

I know which relatives leave me feeling clear and which leave me feeling like I've been dragged through mud. At festival gatherings I position myself carefully — near my uncle who reads and tells quiet stories, not near my cousin whose every sentence is about who got promoted and who didn't.

When I ride the crowded MRT in the morning I can feel the impatience of everyone pressing in — their rushing gets inside me, my jaw tightens, my thoughts start racing about tasks. But if I ate a proper breakfast at home with my parents, there's something in me that holds its shape longer against all that.

I've noticed that when my grandmother is visiting, the whole household runs differently — earlier meals, lighter food, less television. Something calms down. Even my children are less wild. When she leaves and my husband puts the TV on again, I can feel the household texture thicken within a day.

My boss keeps the office fluorescent and loud — radio playing, staff chatting, AC blasting. By lunch my head feels stuffed with cotton. I step out to the hawker centre, eat something simple, sit under a tree for five minutes, and I come back slightly clearer — enough to make it through the afternoon without snapping at the junior staff.

I can feel the pull of different modes rotating through me during a long family dinner — first I'm genuinely warm and present, then something agitated creeps in during the loud toasting and drinking, then by the end I'm dull and just want everyone gone. I've started excusing myself during the middle part so I don't arrive home in the heavy state.

My mother always insisted on warm food in the morning — rice porridge, never cold milk or bread — and as an adult I finally understand why. On days I skip it and grab a cold sandwich from the convenience store, there's a sluggishness by ten that affects how I respond to my colleagues. The warmth in the belly does something to the warmth in my thinking.

Before my daughter's exam week I make sure the house is quiet after eight — no guests, no loud cooking, no arguments. I can feel how the atmosphere we create at home lands in her, and honestly in me too. When I protect that stillness, I sleep better and wake up without the tightness in my chest.

I choose to walk through the temple garden on my way to the bus stop even though it adds ten minutes. Something settles in me — my breath gets deeper, my thoughts slow down. By the time I reach the office I'm operating from a different place than my colleagues who scrolled their phones the whole commute.

I've become that person in the family who polices everyone's food — telling my sister-in-law her fried snacks are making the children dull, scolding my father for eating too late. I can't stop because I can see what it does to them, but now they avoid me and the tension at meals is worse than whatever they were eating.

I monitor the energy of every room I enter — the neighbour's house, the office pantry, even the lift. If I sense heaviness or aggression I leave immediately, sometimes mid-conversation. My colleagues think I'm strange and my husband says I've made our social life impossible.

I've refused to attend my own sister's wedding dinner because it's at night, the food will be heavy, and I know I'll be in a dark, sluggish state for two days after. My mother won't speak to me. But I cannot tolerate what it does to my system — it's not a choice anymore, it's compulsion.

I wake at four a.m. to get my hour of silence before the household stirs, and if my mother-in-law gets up early and starts talking, something in me panics — like the one clean window in my day has been smashed. I've said things to her I'm ashamed of, just to protect that time.

I can't let my children watch anything without vetting it first — even cartoons. I feel the agitation it puts in them, and then I feel it land on me. My husband says I'm controlling the family's every input and he's right, but letting go feels like letting poison through the door.

At office lunches I sit alone because the conversation is always gossip and complaints, and within five minutes of listening I feel my clarity dissolve. My team leader called me in to discuss my 'attitude' but I physically cannot sit in that atmosphere without something in me going dark.

I've cut out almost everyone — old school friends, distant relatives, even my closest cousin — because after each visit I can feel their restlessness or heaviness sticking to me for days. My father says I'm becoming a hermit and losing my humanity, and part of me knows he's right but I can't stop protecting myself.

During Lunar New Year my whole extended family gathers and the noise, the drinking, the heavy food — I spend the entire visit in a state of guarded alertness, managing inputs. Last year I snapped at my aunt for offering my son sweets and the room went silent. I'd gone too far but in the moment it felt like self-defense.

I time every meal, measure every portion, refuse to eat anything my wife cooks unless I know exactly what's in it. She's started crying at dinner. I can see I'm hurting her but the thought of eating without knowing what it will do to my state makes my chest tight.

I've reorganized the entire household — when we eat, what music plays, when the lights go off — and my family just follows because I get agitated if they deviate. My son asked if he could have a friend over after school and I said no without thinking, because I couldn't control what energy that child would bring in.

I eat whatever my mother puts on my plate — oily curries, sweets, heavy rice at ten p.m. — and it never occurs to me that it has anything to do with why I feel thick and slow the next morning. It's just food. Everyone eats like this. I don't understand why my wife keeps saying I should pay attention.

The office is chaotic — people yelling across desks, the radio blaring, someone always eating something pungent — and I just sit in it all day without noticing anything changing in me. By evening I'm exhausted and angry but I have no idea when it started or what caused it.

My wife lights incense in the morning and sits quietly and she says it changes her whole day. I tried it once and felt nothing different — same heaviness, same flat mood, same impatience with the children. I think she's imagining it.

My grandmother always said certain foods make you dull and certain foods make you sharp, and I always thought it was superstition. I eat street food every lunch, drink bubble tea twice a day, and my mind is the same muddled state morning to night. I can't tell what's supposed to be different.

At family gatherings I'm the same person whether it's a quiet prayer ceremony or a loud karaoke night — present in body, absent in feeling. My aunt says I look like I'm sleepwalking through life and honestly that's how it feels, but I don't know what awake would look like.

I scroll my phone until one a.m. every night, lying in bed next to my sleeping wife, and then drag myself up at six wondering why my body feels like cement. It hasn't occurred to me that what I do before sleep creates what I wake up into. My neighbour mentioned it once and I thought she was being preachy.

I work double shifts — the bustle of the kitchen, the shouting, the grease, the heat — come home, eat whatever's left, collapse. People ask how I feel and I say tired. That's the only word I have. There's no before and after, no shift I can point to. It's all just one long blur.

My sister-in-law is always adjusting things — what time she eats, who she visits, what she watches — and I find it excessive. I do whatever comes and I'm fine. Except I'm also irritable most of the time and can't figure out why. Maybe there's something I'm not seeing but I genuinely can't feel it.

During Deepavali I just go along with everything — the heavy sweets, the late nights, the crowds — and by the third day I'm snapping at my children for no reason. My mother says it's because I don't pace myself but I don't even know what she means by that. How is eating a laddu connected to yelling at my son?

My colleague talks about feeling the quality of his commute change depending on whether he reads or watches videos on the train, and it sounds like nonsense to me. I stare out the window or play games — same thing either way. My mood at the office seems random, unrelated to anything I did on the way there.

When I'm driving my kids to school and they're arguing in the back seat, I notice the noise, the steering wheel under my hands, the traffic — all of it solid and real — and at the same time something underneath it all that isn't the car or the road or their voices. I can't name it, but it's what makes the whole morning feel like it's resting on something.

My wife asks how my day was and I tell her about the meeting, the lunch, the commute — but while I'm talking, I'm aware that what actually happened today can't be captured by any of those things. The real thing is behind them. I don't say that part out loud because I'd sound strange.

In the gym, my hands grip the bar and I feel the weight, the muscle burning, and I know this is real. But the aliveness in me that's doing the lifting — that isn't a muscle and it isn't a weight. I hold both at the same time without needing to choose.

At my desk, the spreadsheet, the coffee getting cold, the cursor blinking — all completely here. And whatever is aware of all that is also here but isn't any of those objects. I carry both through the workday without it being a problem.

Walking through the farmers market on Saturday, I touch the peaches, smell the bread, hand over cash — and none of that ever fully exhausts what's actually going on. There's the stuff I can hold, and there's something else that isn't stuff. Both are part of the same morning.

When my mother calls and I hear her voice — familiar, warm, aging — I sense the person I can name and describe, and also something in her that I could never put on paper no matter how long I tried. Both are her. Both are real to me.

During a video call with my team, there's the shared screen, the voices, the agenda. And there's whatever is actually moving through the conversation that none of us are saying. I notice both without needing one to win over the other.

Folding laundry late at night, I feel the warm cotton, the crease under my thumb. The shirt is entirely real. And what makes it real — what makes anything real — isn't itself a shirt. I hold that quietly while I finish the pile.

When I step outside on the first truly warm day of spring and the sun hits my face, I feel the heat on my skin and at the same time something in that warmth that is not temperature. I let both be there. It settles me for the whole morning.

Sitting with my neighbour on his porch after his wife's funeral, I notice the beer in my hand, the creak of the chair, his red eyes. And something else — not grief, not comfort, not anything with a name — holding all of it. I don't try to talk about that part. It's just there, and that's enough.

I can't stop sorting everything into what I can touch and what I can't. At dinner, I'm barely tasting the food because I'm trying to locate the thing behind it — the part that isn't bread or sauce. My partner says I look like I'm somewhere else, and she's right.

In meetings, I keep dismissing what people are actually saying because I'm fixated on something invisible underneath their words. I know it makes me a bad listener. My manager has mentioned it. But the concrete stuff feels like wrapping paper and I can't stop trying to get at what's inside.

My brother invited me to his kid's birthday party and I spent the whole time feeling like the balloons, the cake, the screaming children were a thin screen over something else. I couldn't just be there. I kept reaching past everything visible and missing the actual event.

I lie in bed at night and my body on the mattress feels like a shell. I can't stop trying to sense what's behind the sensation of the sheets, the weight of my limbs. I lose sleep over it — not anxiously, but because I can't let the physical world just be enough.

My friend asked me a simple question — what I wanted for lunch — and I froze because I was caught up in the gap between what lunch is as a thing and what lunch is beyond being a thing. She looked at me like I was broken. It's affecting my daily life now.

I've stopped enjoying hikes because I spend the whole time trying to see through the trees, the rocks, the trail — not past them physically, but into whatever they're an expression of. The defined part of everything has started to feel like an obstacle rather than something to appreciate.

At my son's school play, I could see him on stage in his costume, hear his little voice, but I couldn't connect to that layer. I was stuck trying to reach something formless behind the scene. My wife noticed I didn't clap when everyone else did.

Every object I look at — my coffee mug, my keyboard, my colleague's face — I immediately see two things: the thing itself and what can't be said about it. It used to feel like insight. Now it's like a compulsion. I can't just use the mug.

My therapist asked what I'm feeling and all I could say is what I'm not feeling. Not sad, not happy, not numb. I kept ruling things out but couldn't land anywhere positive. She said it sounded exhausting. It is.

When my girlfriend touches my arm, I register her hand, the pressure, the warmth — and then immediately I'm pulled toward whatever is behind the touch that makes it more than skin on skin. I can't stay in the simple moment. She says I feel far away even when I'm right there.

Everything is just what it is to me. The table is a table. The meeting is a meeting. People talk about sensing something more, something underneath things, and I genuinely don't know what they mean. I just see what's in front of me and that's the whole picture.

My wife told me she feels something sacred in our home that isn't about the furniture or the walls, and I had nothing to say. I see the furniture. I see the walls. That's what home is for me. Whatever else she's pointing at — I can't find it.

At the funeral, everyone seemed to sense something beyond the body in the casket — something still present. I only saw the body. I wanted to feel what they felt, but my experience stopped at what was physically there.

When my friend says a sunset is more than just light and atmosphere, I don't get it. I enjoy the colours, sure. But 'more than' — more than what? The colours are the sunset. I've always felt slightly locked out of whatever layer other people access.

My morning is shower, coffee, drive, desk. Objects, tasks, surfaces. I sometimes wonder if there's supposed to be something else holding all of that together — some kind of invisible thread — but when I reach for it, there's nothing there. Just the next task.

My neighbour tried to explain why he meditates — something about what's behind his thoughts. I hear the words but I live entirely in my thoughts and what I can touch. The idea that there's a formless something I'm missing doesn't connect to anything in my experience.

At work, I'm effective because I deal with what's measurable and concrete. But in quieter moments — waiting for my daughter at practice — people seem to settle into something I can't locate. I just scroll my phone because the bench, the field, the waiting — that's all there is for me.

My college roommate used to say that describing me was impossible because the real me wasn't my habits or my face. I thought that was nonsense. I am my habits and my face. I've never sensed some undefined version of myself that can't be pinned down.

When my kid asks me what's behind the sky, I say more sky, then space, then nothing. I don't have a sense that there's some other kind of answer — something undefinable. The question ends where the physical stuff ends.

People at the networking event were making small talk and someone said they could feel the energy of the room, something invisible connecting everyone. I just felt bodies, noise, and too much cologne. If there's a formless layer to a crowded room, it doesn't register for me at all.

When I'm cooking rice for the family in the evening — the steam rising, the smell of the dal — I know these are real things I can touch and taste. But something else is also in that kitchen that isn't the rice or the steam. I don't need to figure out what it is. Both are just there.

My grandmother is sitting in the courtyard, her hands folding betel leaves — solid, wrinkled, warm. And what she is beyond those hands and that body — I can feel it when I sit near her, but I could never describe it to anyone. I hold both: the grandmother I can see and the one I can't name.

On the crowded train to work, shoulders pressing against me, everyone's breath, the metal pole under my hand — all completely tangible. And running through it, something that isn't any person or any object but is just as real. I ride with both every morning.

During the harvest festival, the oil lamps, the kolam on the floor, the relatives arriving — I feel all of it with my body. And beneath the colours, the noise, the sweets, there's something that isn't any of those things but is somehow what they all rest on. I let it be without chasing it.

When my father gives advice at dinner — his voice, his gestures, the food getting cold — I hear the defined words and I also sense something undefined moving through him that the words can't carry. I don't argue with either part. Both are my father.

Removing my shoes at the door after work, I feel the tile cool under my feet, the weight of my bag coming off my shoulder. And something shifts — like the threshold isn't just between outside and inside, but between what I can point to and what I can't. It happens every evening and I let it pass through me.

My colleague and I were reviewing accounts — numbers on a screen, figures that add up precisely. And still I noticed something in that shared concentration that wasn't a number or a task. I held both without making a fuss about it. The work got done and the other thing stayed quiet beneath it.

When I visit the temple with my mother, there's the stone floor, the incense smoke, the crowd. And there's something that the smoke and the stone are only pointing toward — not the stone itself, not the smoke itself. I carry both back home in the auto-rickshaw without needing to resolve them.

My infant son sleeping on my chest — his weight, his breath, the heat of his body. He's entirely here, physical, defined. And something about him that isn't his body at all is also entirely here. I know both are real the same way I know my own heartbeat.

Drinking tea with my neighbour aunty in the morning — the cup warm in my hands, her gossip about the street — all of it ordinary and concrete. And something in the ordinary that I can only describe by saying what it isn't: not the tea, not the gossip, not the morning air. It doesn't trouble me. I just notice it's there.

My mother-in-law serves me food and I can barely eat because I'm caught up in trying to sense what's behind the rice, the sambar, her hands. The meal goes cold. She thinks I'm ungrateful. I'm not — I just can't stop reaching past the plate toward something without a name.

At my uncle's shop, I'm supposed to be helping with inventory — counting boxes, labelling things. But every object I pick up, I keep getting stuck on what it is beyond being a box. My uncle says I'm too slow. I can't explain that the defined shape of things keeps dissolving for me before I finish counting.

My wife asks me to describe what's wrong and I can only say what it's not. Not tired, not sad, not angry, not bored. She gets frustrated because I never land on a positive answer. But every word I try feels like it covers over the actual thing, which refuses to be named.

During the family prayer in the evening, everyone else seems content with the lamp, the chanting, the ritual. I can't settle into those forms because I keep trying to bypass them — to get at whatever is beyond the flame, beyond the sound. My father says I'm disrespecting the practice, but I'm stuck looking past it.

At the office, my manager presents the new targets — numbers, deadlines, charts. I hear them but something in me keeps insisting that the real thing happening in that room isn't on any slide. I zone out. I've gotten two warnings about attention and I don't know how to explain what's pulling me away.

My friend invited me to her wedding and I stood there watching the garlands, the fire, the silk — all so vivid and defined — and I couldn't feel joy because I was consumed by what none of that could express. Everyone was celebrating the visible. I was lost in what was invisible, and I missed the whole event.

Walking through the vegetable market, vendors shouting prices, the smell of coriander, baskets overflowing — and I freeze because I'm trying to sense what isn't the coriander, what isn't the shout, what can't be weighed on their scales. I come home without buying anything. My mother scolds me.

My body on the mat doing morning stretches — I feel each joint, each pull. But instead of finishing the routine, I keep getting absorbed in what is using the body, what isn't the joint or the muscle. I've lost the ability to just exercise. My sister says I make everything more complicated than it needs to be.

When my grandfather tells stories from his youth — places, names, events — I keep wanting to say 'but that's not it, that's not the real thing.' I can't accept the defined version of his life. He just wants me to listen. I keep reaching past his words for something he isn't saying, and it creates distance between us.

Even at night, lying on the cot on the terrace with the stars visible — I can't rest because I'm gripped by the sense that what I see is only one layer, and the other layer — undefined, shapeless — demands my attention more. I sleep poorly. My energy at work the next day suffers.

For me the world is the world. The jasmine flowers at the temple are jasmine. The traffic is traffic. My grandmother talks about something beyond what we see and I've never once felt what she means. If there's something behind all these things that can't be named, my experience ends at the things themselves.

My father says the lamp we light each evening is pointing toward something you can't hold in your hands. I light the lamp because he asks me to, but for me it's cotton, oil, fire. That's where my experience stops. The rest is just his words.

At my cousin's funeral, everyone spoke about something of him still being present — not his body, not his memory, but something else. I couldn't sense it. I saw the pyre, the ash, the flowers. That was complete for me. Whatever formless thing they were pointing to didn't show up in my experience.

My colleague says that when she looks at the ocean from the office window, she senses something beyond the water. I see the water. I like the water. But 'beyond' it? There's nothing in my experience that answers that. The physical view is the whole thing.

When my mother cooks, she says the food carries something that isn't just taste or nutrition — something you can't describe. I taste good food. I feel full. That's it. I've never sensed an undefined layer in a meal, and when she insists it's there, I feel like I'm missing a sense everyone else has.

On festival mornings, the kolam is just rice powder on the ground. The mango leaves on the door are just leaves. My aunt says they represent something you can't see or touch. For me, what I can't see or touch might as well not be there. I feel flat at festivals because of it.

My neighbour uncle sits on his veranda every evening and says he feels something holding the whole street together that isn't the buildings or the people. I sit with him sometimes and I only feel the concrete bench and the mosquitoes. I wonder what makes him sense a second layer and me only the first.

In the crowded bus to work, everyone packed in tight — bodies, heat, noise. People say there's something connecting us all that isn't physical. But my experience is only the physical: the press of elbows, the diesel smell, the sweat. The idea of a formless connection doesn't land anywhere in my body or mind.

My wife says when she holds my hand, she feels something beyond my skin, beyond even me as a person — something undefinable. I feel her palm, her warmth, her grip. I love her, but whatever undefined thing she's touching, I can't reach it. My experience ends at the hand.

When elders at home talk about how a person is more than their body and their name — that there's something you can only say 'it's not this, not that' about — I nod out of respect but my own life is entirely made of bodies and names. The undefinable part has never shown up for me, and that gap makes me feel alone sometimes.
